title: Improve visibility for long Nix builds during 'havn .' startup
description:
## Summary
When running `havn .`, havn immediately attaches to `nix develop` inside the container. For large first-time builds (example: `codex-0.120.0`), output visibility is poor and status lines are truncated, making it hard to diagnose whether progress is normal or stuck.

## Reproduction
1. Use an env that pulls large dependencies (e.g. `github:jorgengundersen/dev-environments?dir=environments/default`).
2. Run `havn .` in a fresh project/container context.
3. Observe Nix build output while dependencies compile/download.

## Actual behavior
- Nix progress output appears as short/truncated status snippets.
- Example observed line: `[1/67/72 built, 733 copied (6342.7 MiB), 1328.5 MiB DL] building codex-0.120.0 (buildPhase): warning: build` (cut off).
- User has limited insight into what Nix is doing and may kill the session due to uncertainty.

## Expected behavior
- Users can opt into detailed startup/bootstrap visibility for long Nix builds.
- Build logs should be readable, not effectively hidden by progress-bar truncation.
- On failure/interruption, users should have actionable log context without searching many project directories.

## Impact
- Poor DX on first run or cache-miss paths.
- Difficult troubleshooting for long-running derivations.
- Higher likelihood of unnecessary aborts.

## Constraints / logging policy
- Do not scatter logs across project-local files.
- Prefer centralized Nix log persistence in existing Docker Nix volume(s) (e.g. `havn-nix`).
- Ensure operators can inspect logs via container/volume-level paths.

## Candidate fixes
- Add a non-attach prepare mode (e.g. warmup/bootstrap before interactive shell).
- Add flags/config to pass Nix log options (`--log-format raw`, `-L`, verbose levels).
- Ensure Nix build logs are retained in the Nix volume and documented retrieval path is clear.

## Acceptance criteria
- A user can run startup with detailed Nix logs and see full lines for long builds.
- Nix logs for startup builds are retained in Docker Nix volume paths and can be retrieved with documented commands.
- There is a documented troubleshooting path to inspect logs after interruption/failure.
- Default UX remains simple for normal fast-path startups.
notes: Closed after verifying all blocking epics are completed (havn-5r7, havn-c7m, havn-d4b) and acceptance criteria are covered by their shipped child work.

title: align repository governance tests with the new boundary
description:
After removing prose-coupled checks and updating the standard, the remaining repository governance tests should match the new policy. Keep only the checks that verify stable configuration or minimal file presence without turning docs into required CI fixtures.

## Scope
- review remaining internal/ci tests for compliance with the updated standard
- keep or simplify stable checks for workflow/config artifacts where their meaning is not tied to prose phrasing
- remove leftover checks that still indirectly freeze editorial structure without strong justification

## Specs
- specs/test-standards.md Section 5
- specs/quality-gates.md CI

## Wires up
- repository governance coverage
- GitHub workflow and settings verification

## Acceptance
- remaining repository governance tests comply with the documented anti-prose policy
- required CI checks verify stable artifacts only where justified
notes: Removed internal/ci/spec_contracts_test.go to eliminate markdown-prose assertions from required CI tests and kept governance coverage focused on stable repository artifacts (.github workflow/settings). Also added boundary-confidence context assertion to repository settings coverage.

title: update test standards to ban prose-coupled contract tests
description:
The project standards should explicitly say that repository tests must not use spec or doc prose as a required test oracle. Add guidance that documentation can inform behavior tests, but markdown wording itself is not a stable contract fixture.

## Scope
- update specs/test-standards.md
- define the boundary between testing behavior and testing documentation
- state what forms of repository-level structural checks are acceptable, if any

## Specs
- specs/test-standards.md Section 5
- specs/README.md Spec Governance

## Wires up
- future test authoring decisions
- repository governance expectations

## Acceptance
- specs/test-standards.md explicitly forbids required tests that depend on exact prose in specs or derivative docs
- the standard directs contributors toward behavior, machine-readable output, and stable artifacts instead
notes: Updated specs/test-standards.md Section 5 to explicitly ban prose-coupled markdown assertions, clarify acceptable structural checks, and direct tests toward behavior/machine-readable contracts.

title: replace removed prose-coupled checks with behavior or structural verification
description:
Some current prose assertions are standing in for real contract verification. Replace that coverage with tests against behavior, CLI output, typed errors, machine-readable JSON, or stable repository configuration where appropriate.

## Scope
- identify each removed prose-coupled assertion that was protecting a real invariant
- add or strengthen behavior-level or config-level checks in the correct package
- avoid recreating the same dependency in another textual form

## Out of scope
- adding coverage for documentation wording

## Specs
- specs/test-standards.md Section 5

## Wires up
- CLI contract verification
- repository configuration verification
- runtime behavior coverage

## Acceptance
- invariants previously protected only by markdown assertions are covered by code, CLI, or stable config tests where needed
- replacement tests verify behavior or machine-readable contract, not human prose
notes: Replaced string-based CI and repository settings assertions with YAML-parsed structural contract checks. Tests now verify trigger keys, required jobs, dependency edges, and required status-check contexts without coupling to prose or raw formatting.

title: Reconcile project-path home-directory restriction with published startup contract
description:
Startup currently rejects project paths outside the user's home directory, but the in-scope startup/configuration contract does not publish that restriction. This task makes the project-path contract honest for the personal workflow by either documenting the restriction or removing it.

## Scope
- reconcile startup project-path behavior with the published contract
- either document the home-directory restriction clearly or remove it from implementation
- keep the personal single-user workflow explicit and predictable

## Out of scope
- redesigning project discovery semantics beyond this path restriction
- changing unrelated mount or naming behavior

## Specs
- specs/configuration.md §64
- specs/configuration.md §98

## Wires up
- startup path handling that matches the published personal-use contract

## Acceptance
- startup and the published contract agree on whether project paths must live under the user's home directory
- if the restriction remains, it is documented clearly enough for personal use
notes: Resolved startup project-path scope mismatch by documenting current supported behavior in specs/configuration.md: havn [path] startup requires project path under the user's home directory.

title: Make forced Dolt import use a clean replacement strategy
description:
The force-import path currently copies tar content into the shared Dolt data directory without an explicit clean replacement step. This task makes the forced-import overwrite path trustworthy instead of depending on raw copy-into-live-dir behavior.

## Scope
- define and implement a clean replacement strategy for forced import
- preserve the published non-transactional/partial-failure reporting semantics

## Specs
- specs/shared-dolt-server.md §117
- specs/shared-dolt-server.md §157

## Wires up
- safer forced migration behavior for personal shared-Dolt workflows

## Acceptance
- forced import uses a defined replacement path rather than raw copy-into-live-dir semantics
- partial-failure reporting remains truthful about what happened
notes: Implemented clean replacement strategy for forced import: when destination exists and --force is set, import now removes /var/lib/dolt/<db> before copying tar payload. Added red/green coverage asserting rm -rf executes between existence check and post-import verification; preserved non-transactional partial-failure reporting via ImportError wrapping.

title: Make havn dolt import --force use truthful overwrite reporting
description:
Forced import currently reports `overwrote=true` whenever `--force` is used, even if there was no existing destination database. This task makes overwrite reporting truthful so migration output reflects what actually happened.

## Scope
- preserve no-`--force` refusal semantics
- compute overwrite reporting from preexisting destination state, not post-import visibility alone

## Specs
- specs/shared-dolt-server.md §157

## Wires up
- more trustworthy forced-import reporting

## Acceptance
- forced import reports overwrite only when a destination database actually existed before import
notes: Implemented truthful overwrite reporting for force import: Overwrote now reflects destination preexistence, not post-import visibility. Added red/green test TestImport_ForceWhenDestinationMissing_DoesNotReportOverwrote, updated derivative docs and CI spec-contract assertions, and landed in commit 983e803.

title: Align config-mount scope between spec and implementation
description:
Runtime currently restricts config mounts to paths under the user’s home directory, but the in-scope configuration contract does not say that. This task resolves the mismatch so the startup contract is honest for the personal workflow.

## Scope
- either document home-directory-only config mounts as supported behavior or remove that restriction in implementation
- prefer the smallest honest resolution that matches the personal workflow
- keep config-mount behavior explicit and auditable
acceptance_criteria:
- config-mount scope is explicit and consistent between runtime and published docs/specs
- if home-only mounts remain the supported behavior, that restriction is documented clearly enough for personal use
notes: Resolved config-mount scope mismatch by documenting current supported behavior in specs/configuration.md: mounts.config entries must resolve under the user's home directory, and out-of-home entries are validation failures.

title: Validate merged startup port values by numeric range before runtime
description:
Startup currently validates port mapping syntax but not numeric range at the merged config layer. This task makes invalid port values fail deterministically before runtime side effects instead of depending on later Docker/runtime errors.

## Scope
- validate merged `ports` entries and SSH publish-derived host/container values by range
- keep failures expressed as invalid effective config

## Specs
- specs/configuration.md §150
- specs/configuration.md §217

## Wires up
- earlier, clearer failure for invalid startup port config

## Acceptance
- out-of-range startup port values are rejected before container creation
- port validation errors are surfaced as config-validation failures, not late runtime failures
notes: Implemented merged port range validation in config.Validate: host/container ports in ports entries must be 1..65535; added TDD coverage for out-of-range and zero ports. This now fails invalid effective config before container creation.

title: Stop doctor from falling back to default config after effective-config failure
description:
`doctor` currently continues with many host/container checks after effective-config resolution fails, using `config.Default()` instead of the intended project config. That creates misleading output exactly when users most need trustworthy diagnostics.

## Scope
- remove default-config fallback behavior after effective-config failure
- keep config-resolution failure visible in doctor output
- ensure downstream checks only run when their required effective config is actually available

## Specs
- specs/havn-doctor.md §31
- specs/havn-doctor.md §121

## Wires up
- doctor checks that correspond to real intended config
- less misleading troubleshooting output when config is broken

## Acceptance
- doctor does not run default-derived checks after effective-config resolution fails
- config-resolution failures remain visible and actionable in doctor output

title: Make doctor Dolt database detection use robust shared parsing
description:
The doctor `dolt_database` check parses `SHOW DATABASES` output more naively than the Dolt manager already does. This task aligns doctor with the shared parsing contract so database-existence diagnostics stop producing false negatives from tabular output shapes.

## Scope
- reuse or align with the existing shared Dolt database parsing path
- preserve the published `dolt_database` check meaning

## Specs
- specs/havn-doctor.md §97
- specs/shared-dolt-server.md §84

## Wires up
- consistent database detection between doctor and shared-Dolt management

## Acceptance
- doctor and the Dolt manager interpret shared-server database listings consistently
- `dolt_database` no longer misses existing databases because of output formatting alone
notes: Aligned doctor dolt_database parsing with shared Dolt parser by reusing internal/dolt ParseDatabaseNames; added host check test covering tabular SHOW DATABASES output to prevent false negatives.

title: Fix shared Dolt migration and status fidelity for personal use
description:
Fresh Dolt-enabled startup is close, but migration and status still have fidelity gaps that make the shared-Dolt surface less trustworthy in personal use. This epic fixes the most important ones without expanding into broader team or architecture work.

## Scope
- fix `havn dolt import --force` overwrite reporting
- make forced import semantics trustworthy
- fix or narrow the `havn dolt status` runtime-port contract

## Out of scope
- redesigning shared-Dolt architecture
- adding migration workflows beyond the published import/export model
- team-oriented shared-Dolt concerns outside the personal baseline
acceptance_criteria:
- forced import reports overwrite truthfully and uses trustworthy replacement semantics
- status output used in the personal workflow is trustworthy, or the published contract is explicitly narrowed to match supported behavior
close_reason: All child tasks completed; shared Dolt migration/status fidelity tightened

title: Make doctor truthful enough for the personal Dolt-enabled workflow
description:
`havn doctor` is useful, but it currently mixes real checks with misleading fallback behavior, weaker-than-manager Dolt database detection, and some stable-surface drift. This epic makes doctor truthful enough to troubleshoot the personal Dolt-enabled workflow you actually rely on.

## Scope
- remove misleading default-based behavior after config-resolution failure
- make Dolt-related checks match effective config and shared parsing behavior
- reconcile stable-surface drift where emitted checks or severities create misleading noise

## Out of scope
- broad redesign of doctor output or new diagnostic tiers
- expansive new checks unrelated to startup/config/shared-Dolt truthfulness
acceptance_criteria:
- doctor does not continue with misleading default-based checks after effective-config failure
- doctor's Dolt database check uses the same parsing contract as shared-Dolt management
- emitted stable checks and first-run severities are trustworthy for the personal workflow
notes: Completed doctor truthfulness updates: (1) --all now records per-target config-resolution failures as container-tier errors instead of silently skipping those targets, (2) first-run missing base image/network/volumes now report skip (non-alarming) rather than warn, and (3) regression coverage added for both behaviors at the CLI/doctor boundaries.
close_reason: Completed: doctor no longer relies on misleading effective-config fallback behavior, shared-Dolt database detection now uses the shared parser contract, and the stable doctor surface/docs now match emitted behavior

title: Fix revive indent-error-flow lint in volume manager
description: Pre-commit lint while addressing havn-z8x reported revive indent-error-flow in internal/volume/manager.go:38. This is unrelated to doctor config mount expectation docs and should be fixed so lint remains signal-rich.
notes: Refactored EnsureExists in internal/volume/manager.go to avoid else-after-return, satisfying revive indent-error-flow while preserving inspect/create behavior and error wrapping.

title: Fix revive exported-comment lint in doctor checks
description: Pre-commit lint surfaced an unrelated revive warning: internal/doctor/container_checks.go:128 exported type ConfigMountExpectation lacks doc comment or should be unexported. Fix to keep lint signal clean.

title: Remove unused ensureManaged helper in dolt manager
description: Pre-commit lint during havn-nxk.3 reported an unrelated unused function in internal/dolt/manager.go:152 (ensureManaged). Remove or wire it appropriately to restore lint cleanliness.

title: Fix revive lint violation in volume manager
description: Pre-commit lint during havn-nxk.3 reported an unrelated revive warning in internal/volume/manager.go:38 (indent-error-flow). This should be fixed so lint output stays focused on relevant changes.

title: Fix pre-commit lint failure from unused dolt helper
description: Running pre-commit during havn-uhl.1 reported an unused function: internal/dolt/manager.go:152 func (*Manager).ensureManaged is unused (unused). This appears unrelated to config precedence changes and should be fixed so lint remains trustworthy.
notes: Verified fix is present in commit 0891e5e (removed unused ensureManaged helper in internal/dolt/manager.go) and lint passes with 'go tool golangci-lint run ./...'. Local HEAD matches origin/main, so the fix is committed and pushed.

title: Produce tiered working-version readiness assessment
description:
Assess `havn` after roadmap completion against a practical readiness ladder, starting with the minimum level where you can begin using it yourself with the basics working, and then progressing to stronger levels of reliability and confidence.

## Scope
- re-audit the implemented product against specs, docs, runtime behavior, and confidence signals
- define and assess readiness tiers such as:
  - minimum personal-use readiness
  - comfortable day-to-day personal-use readiness
  - collaborator/team-use readiness
  - broader release-readiness
- identify which tier is satisfied, which tier is next, and what concrete gaps remain

## Acceptance
- the audit report explicitly states whether `havn` is ready for minimum personal use
- the audit report defines higher readiness tiers and evaluates current status against each
- the audit report lists remaining blockers to advance from the current tier to the next one
notes: Added tiered readiness assessment to AUDIT_REPORT.md with explicit minimum personal-use verdict (not yet ready), current tier status, and concrete blockers to reach Tier 0 and then Tier 1.

title: Audit working-version readiness after roadmap completion
description:
Run a follow-up audit only after the current roadmap epics are complete, to assess how close `havn` is to a genuinely working version. The audit should answer the practical question “can I start using this for myself yet?” and then grade readiness upward through stronger levels of trust and completeness.

## Scope
- post-roadmap audit after all current epics are complete
- assessment of real working-version readiness rather than only contract alignment
- tiered readiness framing from minimal personal use upward
- explicit identification of what still blocks each readiness tier

## Out of scope
- implementing new fixes during the audit itself
- decomposing follow-up work in advance of the audit findings

## Wires up
- one final readiness checkpoint after the current roadmap lands

## Acceptance
- the epic does not become ready until all current roadmap epics are complete
- the resulting audit answers whether `havn` is ready for minimal personal use
- the resulting audit defines higher readiness tiers beyond that minimum and identifies remaining blockers for each

title: Reduce Docker-specific error and raw-string leakage at the CLI boundary
description:
Further align the CLI boundary with the architecture/code-standards intent by reducing Docker-specific error knowledge and raw-string contract leakage in user-facing CLI orchestration.

## Scope
- Docker-specific error formatting currently owned by `internal/cli`
- cross-package raw-string contract cleanup where stronger havn-native types already exist or are intended
- boundary cleanup that makes CLI code less dependency-aware and more domain-oriented

## Specs
- specs/architecture-principles.md
- specs/code-standards.md

## Wires up
- a thinner, more havn-native CLI boundary with clearer internal contracts

## Acceptance
- CLI error handling is less coupled to Docker-specific concerns
- key cross-package contracts at the CLI boundary rely less on raw strings
- user-facing CLI orchestration better reflects the intended dependency-isolation model

title: Add live shared-Dolt workflow coverage
description:
Add higher-fidelity automated coverage for real shared-Dolt workflows so lifecycle, readiness, status, and data-migration contracts are validated against an actual shared-server path rather than only fakes.

## Scope
- live or higher-fidelity shared-Dolt lifecycle coverage
- real workflow coverage for status/databases/startup-related behavior
- import/export or migration-path coverage where the shared-Dolt contract is runtime-sensitive
- light harness/support work needed to exercise a real shared-Dolt environment

## Specs
- specs/shared-dolt-server.md
- specs/test-standards.md

## Wires up
- stronger runtime confidence for the shared-Dolt subsystem at its actual integration boundary

## Acceptance
- automated tests exercise real shared-Dolt workflows beyond fake-backed unit coverage
- lifecycle/readiness/status behavior is validated against a live or higher-fidelity environment
- shared-Dolt confidence is no longer limited to unit/fake coverage for critical workflow paths

title: Add doctor effective-state behavioral coverage
description:
Add behavioral tests for `havn doctor` that validate the contract users care about: real project targeting, effective-config consumption, runtime-derived checks, and doctor-specific output/exit behavior.

## Scope
- doctor behavioral scenarios against effective project state
- project-targeting and container-selection confidence
- doctor-specific stdout/stderr/exit behavior in realistic flows
- light harness/support work needed to construct effective-state doctor scenarios

## Specs
- specs/havn-doctor.md
- specs/cli-framework.md
- specs/test-standards.md

## Wires up
- confidence that doctor behaves correctly in realistic, spec-relevant scenarios

## Acceptance
- doctor behavior is validated against effective-state scenarios rather than only isolated units
- doctor project targeting and runtime-derived checks are exercised in realistic flows
- doctor output and exit behavior are validated in scenarios users actually encounter
notes: Added behavioral CLI coverage in internal/cli/doctor_test.go for: default scope targeting current project container, effective-state derived Dolt database expectation from project path, and JSON warn-path stream/exit contract (stdout JSON + exit code 1 + empty stderr).

title: Strengthen CI execution of boundary-confidence suites
description:
Wire the new boundary-confidence coverage into CI so the shipped CLI, shared-Dolt, and doctor validation suites actually protect the branch over time.

## Scope
- CI integration for new boundary-confidence suites
- execution-path decisions for boundary-level tests in automation
- alignment between local/CI invocation and the new confidence surface
- regression protection for the new boundary-focused coverage

## Specs
- specs/test-standards.md
- specs/cli-framework.md

## Wires up
- branch protection backed by the new boundary-level confidence suites

## Acceptance
- CI runs the new boundary-confidence suites that matter for shipped behavior
- boundary-focused regressions are more likely to be caught before merge
- local/CI execution expectations for the new confidence surface are documented and aligned
notes: Added dedicated CI boundary-confidence job and required-status-check wiring; introduced make test-boundary-confidence to run shipped-binary CLI contract, doctor CLI behavior, and shared-Dolt integration coverage; updated quality-gates spec and CI contract test accordingly.

title: Add shipped-binary CLI contract coverage
description:
Add end-to-end coverage for the compiled `havn` binary so stdout, stderr, JSON/human selection, command reachability, and exit-code behavior are validated at the actual process boundary.

## Scope
- built-binary CLI invocation coverage
- stdout/stderr contract verification at the process boundary
- exit-code verification at the process boundary
- light harness/support work needed to run boundary-level CLI tests

## Specs
- specs/cli-framework.md
- specs/test-standards.md

## Wires up
- confidence in the shipped CLI process boundary rather than only in-process command execution

## Acceptance
- the compiled `havn` binary is exercised by automated tests for key CLI contract behavior
- stdout/stderr and exit-code behavior are validated at the process boundary
- boundary-level CLI confidence no longer depends only on in-process Cobra execution
notes: Added shipped-binary process-boundary coverage in internal/cli/binary_contract_test.go. Coverage verifies: config show human/json stdout contract, JSON/human error routing to stderr, exit code 1 on command error, and namespaced command reachability via volume help.

title: Repoint doctor, Dolt, config show, build, and volume to shared orchestration
description:
Adopt the extracted orchestration paths across the CLI entry points that currently diverge most. This is the structural adoption step that makes the new boundaries real in command code.

## Scope
- startup adoption of shared orchestration
- doctor adoption of shared project/config orchestration
- Dolt adoption of shared project/config orchestration
- config show, build, and volume adoption where they currently use bespoke helpers

## Specs
- specs/architecture-principles.md
- specs/code-standards.md

## Wires up
- broad command-path reuse of the extracted orchestration layer

## Acceptance
- the targeted CLI entry points depend on shared orchestration rather than bespoke command-local helpers
- duplicated orchestration logic across startup, doctor, Dolt, config show, build, and volume is materially reduced
- command handlers become more wiring-oriented in structure
notes: Completed final orchestration adoption gap by routing havn build image resolution through internal/cli effectiveConfigOrchestrator + projectContext. This removes the last bespoke build config path and ensures build now follows shared precedence/project semantics used by startup, doctor, dolt, config show, and volume. Added CLI regression coverage in internal/cli/build_test.go (TestBuildCommand_UsesProjectConfigImage). Shipped in commit 3ae5e6c on main.

title: Update architectural docs and structural tests for the new boundary
description:
Bring the architectural documentation and structural regression checks into alignment with the extracted orchestration boundary so the repository’s intended shape stays documented and enforced.

## Scope
- architecture/code-standards updates if the documented boundary shape changes
- structural or spec-contract tests that should reflect the new orchestration boundary
- removal of stale references that describe the old thicker CLI shape as intentional

## Specs
- specs/architecture-principles.md
- specs/code-standards.md

## Wires up
- documented and test-backed reinforcement of the extracted boundary

## Acceptance
- architecture docs describe the intended boundary shape after the refactor
- structural/spec tests reflect the updated orchestration arrangement where applicable
- stale descriptions of the old CLI boundary are removed or corrected

title: Centralize effective-config orchestration across CLI entry points
description:
Move command-side effective-config assembly behind one shared orchestration path so startup, config inspection, doctor, and Dolt do not each rebuild config discovery and merge behavior independently.

## Scope
- shared command-side config assembly responsibilities
- removal of duplicate config-loading helpers across CLI entry points
- reuse of common config assembly by startup, config inspection, doctor, and Dolt paths
- architectural consolidation of config orchestration without redefining spec-owned behavior

## Specs
- specs/architecture-principles.md
- specs/code-standards.md
- specs/configuration.md

## Wires up
- one reusable effective-config orchestration path across CLI commands

## Acceptance
- multiple CLI entry points no longer maintain separate effective-config assembly flows
- command-side config orchestration is shared rather than copied
- config assembly responsibilities are structurally centralized even where behavior fixes remain separate
notes: Centralized effective-config assembly into internal/cli/effective_config.go and rewired root/config/doctor/dolt/volume commands to use one orchestrator path; removed duplicate load helpers from dolt.go. Added orchestrator precedence test in internal/cli/effective_config_test.go. Landed in 93e7089.

title: Extract CLI-side runtime translation into reusable orchestration boundaries
description:
Reduce duplicated CLI adapter translation by moving repeated runtime-translation responsibilities behind reusable orchestration boundaries. This keeps dependency isolation while making the CLI layer less repetitive and less behavior-bearing.

## Scope
- repeated CLI-side Docker/domain translation responsibilities
- shared normalization of inspect/exec/result translation where commands currently duplicate it
- boundary cleanup that preserves havn-owned interfaces while reducing command-local translation logic

## Specs
- specs/architecture-principles.md
- specs/code-standards.md

## Wires up
- reusable translation/orchestration layers beneath CLI command handlers

## Acceptance
- repeated runtime translation logic in CLI adapters is materially reduced
- command handlers rely more on shared orchestration and less on repeated translation code
- dependency-isolation boundaries remain intact while CLI duplication decreases

title: Define a shared project-context orchestration path
description:
Create one shared orchestration path for project context so CLI commands stop reconstructing project path, identity, and scope rules independently. This is the architectural foundation for consistent startup, doctor, Dolt, and config-inspection behavior.

## Scope
- shared project-context derivation responsibilities
- startup-compatible project path and identity semantics
- reusable command-side project targeting inputs
- removal of ad hoc command-local project-context derivation paths

## Specs
- specs/architecture-principles.md
- specs/code-standards.md

## Wires up
- one reusable project-context path for multiple CLI commands

## Acceptance
- project-context derivation is no longer duplicated across CLI commands
- startup, doctor, Dolt, and config-related commands can depend on one shared project-context path
- command-local identity/path derivation logic is materially reduced
notes: Implemented shared CLI project-context orchestration in internal/cli/project_context.go and routed root startup, doctor, dolt, and config show through shared context/config helpers to remove duplicated path/identity derivation.

title: Align derivative docs on partial-gap labeling and support status
description:
Make derivative docs consistently reflect the governance rules for `Partial` and other support labels, including calling out concrete current gaps where needed. This focuses on how derivative docs describe reality, not on fixing the underlying runtime behavior.

## Scope
- support-status consistency across derivative docs
- explicit partial-surface labeling where required
- concrete gap callouts for partial derivative guides
- alignment of README/docs wording with the authoritative governance model

## Specs
- specs/README.md
- specs/AGENTS.md
- specs/cli-framework.md
- specs/configuration.md
- specs/havn-doctor.md
- specs/shared-dolt-server.md

## Wires up
- derivative docs that consistently defer to authoritative specs without overstating current support

## Acceptance
- derivative docs use support labels consistently where the governance model requires them
- partial derivative guides call out concrete current gaps instead of reading like fully landed contracts
- derivative docs do not blur intended contract and current implemented behavior
notes: Updated derivative guides with a concrete 'Current partial-support gaps' section (CLI reference, configuration guide, Dolt/beads guide) and added CI coverage in internal/ci/spec_contracts_test.go to prevent regressions.

title: Expand CI checks for corpus ownership and support-label alignment
description:
Strengthen the automated checks that protect the published corpus so ownership boundaries and support-label consistency are harder to regress. This is about corpus alignment enforcement, not runtime testing.

## Scope
- CI/spec-test coverage for derivative docs currently outside the existing checks
- automated enforcement for ownership-boundary expectations where practical
- automated enforcement for support-label consistency across the published corpus
- regression protection for governance/derivative alignment rules

## Specs
- specs/README.md
- specs/AGENTS.md
- specs/cli-framework.md

## Wires up
- better regression protection for published contract ownership and labeling consistency

## Acceptance
- automated checks cover more of the published docs in scope
- support-label and ownership-alignment regressions are more likely to be caught automatically
- CI coverage better matches the governance expectations for the documentation corpus
notes: Added CI contract coverage requiring doctor derivative docs to include a concrete partial-support gaps section and explicit container_tier skip-check gap callout; updated doctor troubleshooting guide accordingly.

title: Consolidate spec-governance rules into one canonical source
description:
Make the governance rules for authoritative specs, derivative docs, overview responsibilities, and support-status labels live in one canonical place. This reduces drift in the ownership model itself.

## Scope
- canonical home for governance rules
- removal or narrowing of duplicated governance text elsewhere
- clear reference path for authority-level and support-label definitions
- consistency of shared vocabulary and cross-spec invariants

## Specs
- specs/README.md
- specs/AGENTS.md

## Wires up
- one clear source of truth for how the spec corpus governs itself

## Acceptance
- governance rules have one canonical home
- duplicated governance text elsewhere is removed or clearly reduced to a pointer
- readers can identify the governing ownership model without ambiguity
notes: Consolidated governance ownership by making specs/README.md the canonical source and reducing specs/AGENTS.md to a pointer. Added internal/ci/spec_contracts_test.go coverage to enforce canonical-source + no-duplication invariants.

title: Reduce status-bearing duplication in the overview spec
description:
Refocus the overview spec on product framing and reading order, reducing duplicated command/status material that belongs to subsystem authorities. This keeps overview from becoming a second quasi-authoritative command contract.

## Scope
- overview-vs-authoritative boundary cleanup
- reduction of duplicated command/status summaries where they are not needed for framing
- stronger overview deferral to subsystem specs
- preservation of useful navigation/reading-order context without re-owning behavior

## Specs
- specs/havn-overview.md
- specs/README.md
- specs/cli-framework.md
- specs/configuration.md
- specs/shared-dolt-server.md

## Wires up
- clearer separation between framing docs and authoritative contracts

## Acceptance
- the overview spec remains useful for framing and navigation
- status-bearing subsystem contract details are reduced where they duplicate authorities
- overview no longer acts like a second command/status owner in practice

title: Consolidate canonical contract ownership
description:
Finish the spec/doc ownership cleanup so each major behavior has one authoritative home and derivative docs consistently defer to it. This is the roadmap item for preventing future drift in config, CLI, support status, and shared Dolt docs.

## Scope
- spec ownership boundaries
- overview vs authoritative spec responsibilities
- derivative doc alignment
- support-status consistency across published docs

## Specs
- specs/README.md
- specs/AGENTS.md
- specs/cli-framework.md
- specs/configuration.md
- specs/shared-dolt-server.md
- specs/havn-overview.md

## Wires up
- one authoritative contract source per subsystem

## Acceptance
- major user-visible behaviors have clear canonical ownership
- derivative docs no longer redefine authoritative contracts
- support labels and command semantics are consistent across the published corpus

title: Raise confidence at the shipped CLI boundary
description:
Add stronger confidence where users actually experience `havn`: built-binary CLI behavior, live shared-Dolt paths, and doctor behavior against real effective state. This epic focuses on closing the gap between internal library confidence and user-contract confidence.

## Scope
- end-to-end CLI tests for stdout/stderr/exit codes
- live or higher-fidelity shared Dolt integration coverage
- behavioral doctor coverage
- contract-focused validation at the shipped boundary

## Specs
- specs/cli-framework.md
- specs/havn-doctor.md
- specs/shared-dolt-server.md
- specs/test-standards.md

## Wires up
- stronger validation of published user-facing contracts

## Acceptance
- key CLI contracts are exercised through the built binary
- shared Dolt critical flows have higher-fidelity coverage
- doctor behavior is validated against effective-state scenarios

title: Extract shared orchestration from CLI boundary
description:
Reduce the thickness of `internal/cli` by moving project-context derivation, config resolution, and runtime translation behind reusable orchestration services. This epic is about architecture: making startup, doctor, Dolt commands, and config inspection reuse the same core logic.

## Scope
- project-context derivation
- effective-config assembly
- runtime translation and adapter boundaries
- reuse across command entry points

## Specs
- specs/architecture-principles.md
- specs/code-standards.md

## Wires up
- shared orchestration services used by multiple CLI commands

## Acceptance
- CLI commands become thinner and more wiring-focused
- duplicated derivation and translation logic is removed from command handlers
- startup, doctor, Dolt, and config inspection share reusable orchestration paths

title: Align user-facing docs with authoritative specs and status labels
description:
Update user-facing docs after the spec hardening work so they describe the product consistently with the authoritative specs and clearly label implemented versus planned behavior.

## Scope
- update `README.md`, `docs/cli-reference.md`, `docs/configuration-guide.md`, and `docs/dolt-beads-guide.md` to match authoritative specs
- remove overstated support claims and add status labeling where needed
- make docs point back to authoritative specs for detailed normative behavior

## Out of scope
- code changes
- adding brand-new product docs unrelated to audited drift

## Specs
- specs/README.md
- specs/cli-framework.md
- specs/havn-overview.md
- specs/havn-doctor.md
- specs/shared-dolt-server.md

## Wires up
- user-facing docs that accurately reflect the strengthened spec corpus
- less support-status drift between docs and implementation

## Acceptance
- key user-facing docs no longer claim unsupported behavior as implemented
- docs align with authoritative spec ownership and status conventions
- readers can distinguish normative spec detail from user guidance

title: Consolidate shared Dolt spec ownership and safety semantics
description:
Consolidate shared Dolt contract ownership into `specs/shared-dolt-server.md` and strengthen its lifecycle and data-safety semantics. This removes drift between overview and Dolt-specific docs while making safety expectations explicit.

## Scope
- make `specs/shared-dolt-server.md` authoritative for shared Dolt lifecycle, readiness, ownership, import and export, and migration behavior
- clarify overwrite, rollback, project-identity, and partial-failure semantics
- reduce duplicate shared-Dolt contract text in overview and docs

## Out of scope
- redesigning the shared Dolt feature
- implementing safety fixes in code

## Specs
- specs/shared-dolt-server.md
- specs/havn-overview.md
- docs/dolt-beads-guide.md

## Wires up
- one authoritative shared-Dolt contract with explicit safety semantics
- clearer doc boundaries for user guidance versus normative behavior

## Acceptance
- shared Dolt lifecycle and data-safety rules are defined in one authoritative spec
- overview and docs point to that authority instead of restating conflicting detail
- ownership, readiness, import and export, and migration semantics are explicit and reviewable

title: Define spec status and authority conventions in specs README
description:
Add a small spec-governance section to `specs/README.md` so readers can tell which documents are authoritative and how to interpret implemented, partial, planned, and derivative material.

## Scope
- define authoritative spec versus derivative doc roles
- define support-status labels and how they should be used
- define how new specs are added to the corpus and linked from the index

## Out of scope
- rewriting every spec in this task
- enforcing status mechanically in CI

## Specs
- specs/README.md

## Wires up
- a lightweight governance model for the spec corpus
- clearer reading order and trust model for maintainers and agents

## Acceptance
- `specs/README.md` defines authority and status conventions
- readers can identify whether a doc is normative or derivative
- new major specs have a clear place in the indexed corpus

title: Add cross-spec invariants and contract vocabulary guide
description:
Create a small shared vocabulary and invariant set so core terms and expectations mean the same thing across specs. This reduces semantic drift even when multiple specs mention related behavior.

## Scope
- define common terms such as global, persistent, root-only, best-effort, effective config, stdout and stderr contract, and machine-readable JSON contract
- define cross-spec invariants such as doctor using startup config semantics and runtime translation preserving resolved values
- add references from relevant specs to the shared vocabulary and invariants section

## Out of scope
- restating every command or subsystem contract in full
- implementation changes

## Specs
- specs/README.md
- specs/architecture-principles.md
- specs/cli-framework.md

## Wires up
- shared vocabulary for future spec edits
- fewer contradictions caused by different words for the same contract

## Acceptance
- core contract terms have one documented meaning
- at least the main CLI, config, doctor, and runtime invariants are written down
- relevant specs reference the shared vocabulary instead of redefining terms ad hoc

title: Reconcile CLI contract ownership and flag semantics
description:
Reconcile CLI contract ownership across `specs/cli-framework.md`, `specs/havn-overview.md`, and user-facing CLI docs so command tree, flag scope, output semantics, and support status are described consistently.

## Scope
- decide which spec owns command-tree, flag-scope, and output-contract detail
- align terminology for global, persistent, and root-only flags
- reconcile command support claims such as completion and command-level output behavior

## Out of scope
- implementing missing CLI features
- changing command behavior in code

## Specs
- specs/cli-framework.md
- specs/havn-overview.md
- docs/cli-reference.md

## Wires up
- one coherent CLI contract readers and implementers can trust
- cleaner separation between framework rules and user-facing command reference

## Acceptance
- flag scope terminology is consistent across CLI specs and docs
- command support claims no longer contradict the implemented, partial, and planned status model
- stdout, stderr, and JSON contract ownership is clearly assigned

title: Clarify doctor spec against shared runtime semantics
description:
Strengthen `specs/havn-doctor.md` so it explicitly depends on the same effective config, project context, and runtime resolution used by startup. This avoids future drift where doctor documents checks against a different world than the main product flow.

## Scope
- define that doctor uses the same effective config and project-identity rules as startup unless explicitly stated otherwise
- clarify `--all`, skip semantics, per-check prerequisites, and output behavior
- clarify what doctor verifies directly versus what it reports from derived or runtime state

## Out of scope
- adding new doctor checks in code
- redesigning observability beyond doctor's published contract

## Specs
- specs/havn-doctor.md
- specs/havn-overview.md
- specs/cli-framework.md

## Wires up
- a trustworthy doctor contract aligned with runtime behavior
- clearer boundaries for doctor-specific logic versus shared startup and config logic

## Acceptance
- the doctor spec explicitly references shared config and runtime semantics
- `--all`, skip behavior, check inputs, and output contracts are unambiguous
- doctor no longer reads as an isolated subsystem with separate config semantics

title: Reduce havn-overview to product overview and pointers
description:
Refocus `specs/havn-overview.md` into an overview spec that explains product shape, main workflows, and links to authoritative subsystem specs, instead of duplicating detailed contracts. This reduces drift and makes ownership clear.

## Scope
- remove or condense detailed subsystem contract text now owned by dedicated specs
- keep overview-level workflows, command map, and product framing
- add clear pointers to authoritative specs for config, doctor, Dolt, and CLI details

## Out of scope
- inventing new product behavior
- replacing dedicated subsystem specs

## Specs
- specs/havn-overview.md
- specs/README.md
- specs/cli-framework.md
- specs/havn-doctor.md
- specs/shared-dolt-server.md

## Wires up
- a cleaner spec hierarchy with less duplicated contract text
- an overview that can stay stable while subsystem specs evolve

## Acceptance
- `specs/havn-overview.md` no longer acts as the hidden authority for detailed config or subsystem contracts
- overview sections point readers to authoritative specs for detailed behavior
- duplicated CLI, doctor, and Dolt contract text is substantially reduced

title: Author canonical configuration spec
description:
Author a dedicated configuration spec so config behavior no longer lives implicitly inside `specs/havn-overview.md`. This creates one authoritative contract for file discovery, precedence, env overrides, flag overrides, flake resolution, effective-config output, and provenance metadata.

## Scope
- create a dedicated config spec covering config sources, precedence, merge behavior, defaults, and output contracts
- define authoritative behavior for `--config`, project and global config discovery, env overrides, and `havn config show`
- move detailed config contract ownership out of overview prose

## Out of scope
- changing implementation behavior
- rewriting unrelated CLI command specs

## Specs
- specs/architecture-principles.md §8
- specs/havn-overview.md
- specs/cli-framework.md

## Wires up
- one source of truth for configuration semantics
- a reference contract for startup, doctor, and config inspection behavior

## Acceptance
- a dedicated config spec exists and is cited from `specs/README.md`
- config precedence, defaults, discovery, and provenance metadata are defined in one place
- overlapping config detail in `specs/havn-overview.md` is reduced to pointers or summary-level wording

title: Strengthen spec corpus after audit
description:
Strengthen the havn spec corpus after the project-health audit so the specs become a reliable contract set before implementation follow-up begins. This epic focuses on ownership, consistency, terminology, and support-status clarity across specs and user-facing docs.

## Scope
- establish authoritative ownership for major product contracts
- remove duplicated or contradictory behavior definitions across specs
- define status and authority conventions for implemented versus planned behavior
- align user-facing docs to the strengthened spec corpus

## Out of scope
- implementing code fixes discovered by the audit
- changing product direction beyond clarifying current intended contracts

## Specs
- specs/README.md
- specs/architecture-principles.md
- specs/cli-framework.md
- specs/havn-overview.md
- specs/havn-doctor.md
- specs/shared-dolt-server.md

## Wires up
- a stable contract baseline for future implementation and audit follow-up work
- clearer boundaries between authoritative specs and derivative docs

## Acceptance
- authoritative ownership is explicit for config, CLI, doctor, Dolt, and overview material
- duplicated or contradictory contracts are reduced or removed
- user-facing docs no longer overstate implementation status relative to specs

title: Synthesize audit findings into AUDIT_REPORT.md
description:
Consolidate the full audit graph into one root-level `AUDIT_REPORT.md` that gives maintainers a durable, evidence-backed view of project health. This task exists to turn slice-level investigations into a single artifact that can drive follow-up planning without re-reading every issue.

## Scope
- synthesize findings from all audit child tasks into `AUDIT_REPORT.md` in the project root
- include executive summary, audit scope, method, findings by slice, cross-cutting themes, and recommended follow-up issue candidates
- include citations back to specs, docs, code, and audit issues so readers can verify claims quickly
- make the report usable both as a human handoff artifact and as input for future issue-graph decomposition

## Out of scope
- implementing fixes discovered by the audit
- duplicating raw research notes when a concise evidence-backed synthesis is enough

## Specs
- specs/README.md
- specs/architecture-principles.md
- specs/code-standards.md
- specs/test-standards.md
- specs/quality-gates.md
- specs/cli-framework.md
- specs/havn-overview.md
- specs/base-image.md
- specs/havn-doctor.md
- specs/shared-dolt-server.md

## Wires up
- the final audit artifact requested for this project
- a concise input document for creating follow-up fix epics and tasks

## Acceptance
- `AUDIT_REPORT.md` exists at the project root and synthesizes all completed audit slices
- the report groups findings by domain and cross-cutting theme instead of duplicating issue text verbatim
- each major finding is traceable to cited evidence and the report ends with clearly bounded follow-up recommendations

title: Audit security-sensitive surfaces and operational resilience
description:
Audit the parts of havn where a contract bug could expose host resources, mishandle secrets, weaken containment, or hide operational failure. This task exists to add a security and resilience lens that is broader than code style and narrower than redesign work.

## Scope
- assess SSH exposure, SSH agent forwarding, authorized_keys handling, host env passthrough, mount read-only/read-write fidelity, and host-path trust boundaries
- assess self-healing versus failure masking in image, network, volume, and service setup paths
- assess blast radius and containment in multi-unit or shared-resource operations, including persistent data flows and best-effort behavior
- identify risk areas where current behavior could surprise users even if it is partially functional

## Out of scope
- redesigning the product's security model or adding future hardening features
- duplicating lower-level behavioral audits except where the security or resilience angle changes the conclusion

## Specs
- specs/architecture-principles.md
- specs/havn-overview.md
- specs/base-image.md
- specs/havn-doctor.md
- specs/shared-dolt-server.md

## Wires up
- a focused audit of exposure, containment, and failure-mode risk across the codebase
- evidence for security-sensitive or resilience-sensitive gaps that should become follow-up issues

## Acceptance
- the audit identifies concrete exposure, containment, or failure-mode risks with citations
- findings distinguish deliberate spec-defined behavior from accidental or incomplete behavior
- the output clearly bounds which follow-up items are security-sensitive, resilience-sensitive, or both
close_reason: Completed audit slice and captured findings in AUDIT_REPORT.md

title: Audit CI, quality gates, and merge confidence
description:
Audit the automation that is supposed to prevent drift from reaching reviewers and protected branches. This task exists to assess whether local hooks, make targets, CI workflows, and branch protection actually enforce the quality contract the project claims to rely on.

## Scope
- compare Makefile targets, lint/format/build/test gates, hook behavior, GitHub Actions workflows, and branch protection against the quality-gates and test standards specs
- assess parity between local and CI enforcement, including whether gates verify or silently rewrite project state
- identify false-green and false-red risks, missing required automation, and reproducibility or portability blind spots
- note release-readiness gaps where the repository claims stronger delivery confidence than the automation currently proves

## Out of scope
- auditing test content quality beyond what is needed to judge the gates themselves
- implementing new workflows or settings

## Specs
- specs/quality-gates.md
- specs/test-standards.md
- specs/code-standards.md
- specs/architecture-principles.md

## Wires up
- a delivery-confidence assessment for local and CI quality gates
- evidence for mismatches between documented and enforced automation

## Acceptance
- the audit identifies where documented gates and actual enforcement differ, with citations
- false-green, false-red, and dirty-tree risks are clearly described and bounded
- the output highlights missing automation that would materially reduce review burden or audit follow-up noise
close_reason: Completed audit slice and captured findings in AUDIT_REPORT.md

title: Audit test quality and contract coverage
description:
Audit whether the test suite gives reliable confidence in havn's published behavior and structural contracts. This task exists to evaluate depth and quality of verification, not just test count or file presence.

## Scope
- assess unit, integration, and any end-to-end coverage across major domains such as CLI, config, lifecycle, Docker wrappers, Dolt, doctor, mounts, and naming
- evaluate black-box discipline, use of fakes and mocks, contract-vs-implementation coupling, and alignment with test standards
- identify important user-visible or data-sensitive paths that are weakly tested or only fake-backed
- note flaky-test risk, skip behavior, and blind spots that reduce self-verification confidence

## Out of scope
- CI workflow mechanics except where they directly affect test confidence
- writing new tests or proposing detailed TDD plans

## Specs
- specs/test-standards.md
- specs/architecture-principles.md
- specs/cli-framework.md
- specs/shared-dolt-server.md
- specs/havn-doctor.md

## Wires up
- a coverage-depth map for major domains and contracts
- evidence for where the current test suite over-couples to implementation or under-covers important behavior

## Acceptance
- the audit maps major domains to current verification depth and confidence level with citations
- contract gaps are separated from style preferences or raw coverage-number debates
- output identifies the highest-value missing or weakly verified behaviors without prescribing implementation sequencing
close_reason: Completed audit slice and captured findings in AUDIT_REPORT.md

title: Audit architecture boundaries, dependency isolation, and code quality
description:
Audit the codebase for conformance to the architecture and code standards that are meant to keep havn maintainable for both humans and agents. This task exists to find structural drift that increases blast radius, weakens discoverability, or lets dependency details leak into domain code.

## Scope
- assess package boundaries, one-job decomposition, import direction, and domain-first organization
- audit dependency isolation, wrapper leakage, compile-time interface assertions, error translation, and named-type usage where standards call for them
- assess logger usage, structured attribute conventions, and other code-quality patterns defined in code standards
- identify majority-pattern drift that weakens the codebase as a prompt for future contributors and agents

## Out of scope
- re-auditing user-visible command or workflow behavior already covered by other tasks except as supporting evidence
- prescribing implementation fixes beyond documenting bounded follow-up work

## Specs
- specs/architecture-principles.md
- specs/code-standards.md
- specs/cli-framework.md

## Wires up
- a structural quality assessment tied to explicit architecture and code-standard rules
- a drift list for boundary violations and inconsistent codebase signals

## Acceptance
- findings are tied to explicit architectural or coding-standard expectations with citations
- the audit distinguishes isolated local defects from cross-cutting structural patterns
- output identifies which issues are about package boundaries, dependency isolation, typing, error handling, logging, or agent-signal drift
close_reason: Completed audit slice and captured findings in AUDIT_REPORT.md

title: Audit doctor diagnostics and observability truthfulness
description:
Audit whether `havn doctor` is checking the right things, reporting them accurately, and reflecting the same runtime/config semantics used by the rest of the product. This task exists to verify diagnostic trustworthiness rather than just raw feature presence.

## Scope
- compare the doctor spec and troubleshooting docs against actual host and container checks, identifiers, ordering, skip behavior, and exit codes
- audit whether doctor uses effective config and shared primitives consistently with startup logic
- assess recommendation quality, false-positive/false-negative risk, and whether verbose and JSON modes preserve the documented contracts
- identify high-value missing checks that would materially improve diagnostic trust without expanding into implementation work

## Out of scope
- adding or changing doctor checks in code
- general observability architecture outside the doctor command unless needed to explain a finding

## Specs
- specs/havn-doctor.md
- specs/havn-overview.md
- specs/cli-framework.md
- specs/architecture-principles.md

## Wires up
- a per-check coverage and truthfulness matrix for diagnostics
- evidence for configuration drift, duplicate logic, or misleading diagnostics

## Acceptance
- each documented doctor check is classified with evidence as MET, PARTIAL, MISSING, or DIVERGENT
- the audit identifies where doctor and startup/runtime logic disagree in ways that could mislead users
- any suggested new checks are framed as follow-up opportunities, not implementation work inside this task
close_reason: Completed audit slice and captured findings in AUDIT_REPORT.md

title: Audit shared Dolt and beads integration contracts
description:
Audit the shared Dolt server model end to end, including lifecycle management, database provisioning, migration flows, and beads integration. This task exists to assess whether the current shared-server story is reliable, safe for user data, and faithful to the published contract.

## Scope
- compare `havn dolt start|stop|status|databases|drop|connect|import|export` behavior against the shared-Dolt and overview specs
- audit server readiness, ownership checks, database defaulting, env injection for beads, and import/export semantics
- audit migration detection, project-identity safety, overwrite behavior, and partial-failure handling where persistent data is involved
- include docs drift in `docs/dolt-beads-guide.md` where it affects shared-server expectations

## Out of scope
- general root startup behavior not specific to Dolt integration
- redesigning Dolt auth or TLS beyond documenting current gaps and risk

## Specs
- specs/shared-dolt-server.md
- specs/havn-overview.md
- specs/architecture-principles.md
- specs/code-standards.md

## Wires up
- a shared-Dolt contract matrix spanning command surfaces, startup integration, and migration safety
- evidence for data-safety or lifecycle-ordering gaps that need follow-up issues

## Acceptance
- the audit documents how each major shared-Dolt contract currently behaves versus spec
- migration and persistent-data risk areas are identified with clear boundaries and citations
- findings distinguish CLI-surface drift, domain-logic gaps, wrapper/runtime issues, and documentation drift
close_reason: Completed audit slice and captured findings in AUDIT_REPORT.md

title: Audit startup lifecycle, mounts, and base image workflow
description:
Audit the root `havn [path]` startup and attach flow from path resolution through container attach, including the base-image contract and all runtime inputs that should survive the config-to-runtime boundary. This task exists to judge whether the most important user workflow is coherent, complete, and faithful to spec.

## Scope
- compare the documented startup sequence against implementation step by step
- audit project-path validation, name derivation, running-container attach behavior, and exit-code behavior
- audit image build and missing-image recovery against the shared base-image contract
- audit network and volume self-healing, mount resolution, env injection, resource propagation, SSH port handling, and attach/init behavior
- identify where values resolved by config or mount logic are dropped, transformed incorrectly, or ignored before runtime

## Out of scope
- shared Dolt lifecycle details except where they are part of the root startup path
- broader code-style concerns not specific to lifecycle behavior

## Specs
- specs/havn-overview.md
- specs/base-image.md
- specs/architecture-principles.md
- specs/code-standards.md

## Wires up
- a step-by-step lifecycle gap analysis for the main product workflow
- evidence for boundary-loss issues between config, domain orchestration, and Docker runtime translation

## Acceptance
- each major startup step is assessed with cited evidence against the documented contract
- dropped or mistranslated runtime surfaces such as mounts, ports, env, or init behavior are explicitly identified
- findings separate root-command, domain-orchestration, mount/name, and Docker-wrapper problems so follow-up work stays bounded
close_reason: Completed audit slice and captured findings in AUDIT_REPORT.md

title: Audit CLI and config contracts against implementation
description:
Audit the published CLI and configuration contracts against the actual command tree and config-resolution behavior. This task focuses on user-visible surfaces and stable machine-readable contracts so the audit catches broken scripts, stale docs, and contract drift early.

## Scope
- compare command tree, flags, positional arguments, help surfaces, version, and completion behavior against the CLI and overview specs
- audit stdout/stderr separation, JSON result shapes, JSON error shapes, and exit-code expectations where documented
- audit config precedence across defaults, global config, project config, env vars, and flags, including `--config` handling and flake resolution
- verify `havn config show` behavior, including effective values and `source` provenance metadata
- include supporting docs in `docs/cli-reference.md` and `docs/configuration-guide.md`

## Out of scope
- deep container-runtime internals except where they change the published CLI or config contract
- shared Dolt internals beyond CLI/config surfaces that expose them

## Specs
- specs/cli-framework.md
- specs/havn-overview.md
- specs/code-standards.md
- specs/architecture-principles.md

## Wires up
- a command-by-command contract matrix for CLI and config behavior
- evidence for user-visible drift between published contracts and actual behavior

## Acceptance
- the audit records MET, PARTIAL, MISSING, or DIVERGENT status for major CLI and config contracts with citations
- user-visible contract mismatches are separated from purely internal implementation concerns
- the output identifies which gaps belong to command wiring, config resolution, docs drift, or output formatting
close_reason: Completed audit slice and captured findings in AUDIT_REPORT.md

title: Audit spec coherence and documentation drift
description:
Review the spec corpus and supporting docs as a contract set before deeper implementation audits proceed. This task establishes a reliable source-of-truth baseline, surfaces contradictions, and identifies where docs claim implemented behavior that specs mark as planned or vice versa.

## Scope
- compare specs in `specs/` against each other for contradictions, overlap, stale language, and missing cross-references
- compare user-facing docs in `docs/` and `README.md` against the spec corpus for drift in claimed behavior and support status
- identify unclear or underspecified areas that downstream implementation-gap tasks must treat carefully
- produce an evidence-backed baseline that distinguishes intentional design, planned behavior, and stale documentation

## Out of scope
- verifying runtime behavior in code except where documentation explicitly claims current implementation status
- proposing implementation fixes beyond documenting contract drift

## Specs
- specs/README.md
- specs/architecture-principles.md
- specs/cli-framework.md
- specs/havn-overview.md
- specs/base-image.md
- specs/havn-doctor.md
- specs/shared-dolt-server.md

## Wires up
- a normalized audit baseline for downstream gap-analysis tasks
- evidence for spec/spec and spec/docs contradictions that should not be silently propagated

## Acceptance
- conflicting, duplicated, stale, or ambiguous contracts across specs and docs are identified with citations
- the output clearly distinguishes implemented behavior, planned behavior, and unclear intent
- downstream audit tasks can reference this task as the documentation baseline instead of re-deciding source-of-truth questions
close_reason: Completed audit slice and captured findings in AUDIT_REPORT.md

title: Audit project health across specs, implementation, and delivery
description:
Create a full-project audit plan that investigates how well havn matches its specs and where the codebase needs correction, hardening, or clarification. The goal is a decomposed audit graph that produces one root-level `AUDIT_REPORT.md` with evidence-backed findings and follow-up recommendations.

## Scope
- cover spec coherence across the spec corpus
- compare published contracts to implementation across CLI, config, lifecycle, Dolt, doctor, and docs
- assess code quality, test quality, business-logic health, operational resilience, observability, and security-sensitive surfaces
- synthesize findings into `AUDIT_REPORT.md` in the project root

## Out of scope
- implementing fixes discovered by the audit
- changing product direction beyond documenting contradictions or unclear intent

## Specs
- specs/README.md
- specs/architecture-principles.md
- specs/code-standards.md
- specs/test-standards.md
- specs/quality-gates.md
- specs/cli-framework.md
- specs/havn-overview.md
- specs/base-image.md
- specs/havn-doctor.md
- specs/shared-dolt-server.md

## Wires up
- a bounded audit issue graph spanning product contracts, implementation health, and delivery safeguards
- a final synthesis artifact in `AUDIT_REPORT.md`

## Acceptance
- audit work is decomposed into bounded child tasks with explicit objectives and boundaries
- the graph covers spec coherence, implementation gaps, code quality, test quality, business-logic health, and additional high-value audit dimensions
- completion of the graph results in a root-level `AUDIT_REPORT.md` that consolidates findings and recommended follow-up work
close_reason: Completed audit epic with all child tasks closed and final report created

title: Add Docker-gated integration-test CI stage
description:
Integration tests are already part of the quality-gate contract, but they need a separate CI stage because they depend on Docker and may have different execution requirements than unit tests.

## Scope
- Add a CI job or stage for make test-integration
- Gate execution on Docker-capable runners or equivalent environment support
- Keep integration failures visible separately from unit-test failures

## Specs
- specs/quality-gates.md CI
- specs/test-standards.md integration tests

## Wires up
- Repository CI enforcement for integration tests

## Acceptance
- CI can run the integration-test stage in an environment that satisfies its Docker requirements

title: Enforce merge-blocking CI checks
description:
The specs say CI failures block merge. Make that true in the chosen CI provider by enforcing the required status checks for the pipeline introduced under this epic.

## Scope
- Identify the checks that must be required for merge
- Configure merge-blocking enforcement for those checks
- Keep the enforcement aligned with the documented quality-gate contract

## Specs
- specs/quality-gates.md CI
- specs/test-standards.md CI integration
- specs/architecture-principles.md CI as backpressure

## Wires up
- Merge protection based on CI results

## Acceptance
- The repository treats the core CI checks as merge-blocking rather than informational

Implemented merge-blocking CI enforcement via .github/settings.yml branch protection for main requiring quality-gates and integration-tests. Added internal/ci/repository_settings_test.go to assert required status-check contract in repo.

title: Add CI workflow for core quality gates
description:
Local quality gates already exist; this issue adds repository CI so those gates run automatically on every push and pull request instead of depending only on developer machines.

## Scope
- Add CI workflow configuration for fmt, lint, test, and build
- Mirror the existing local quality-gate contract
- Run on both pushes and pull requests

## Specs
- specs/quality-gates.md CI
- specs/test-standards.md CI integration
- specs/code-standards.md CI formatting and lint requirements

## Wires up
- Repository CI enforcement for core Go quality gates

## Acceptance
- CI runs the core quality gates automatically on pushes and pull requests
notes: Implemented GitHub Actions CI at .github/workflows/ci.yml with separate jobs: quality-gates runs make check on push/pull_request; integration-tests runs make test-integration after quality-gates. Added internal/ci/workflow_test.go to enforce trigger/job contract.

title: Integration-test daemon, image, copy, and exec wrappers
description:
Add real-daemon integration coverage for the remaining non-interactive Docker wrappers. Daemon, image, copy, and non-interactive exec paths all sit at external-system boundaries and should be verified in practice.

## Scope
- Cover daemon ping and info success paths
- Cover image inspect, exists, and build success paths
- Cover copy-to and copy-from container success paths
- Cover non-interactive container exec success paths

## Specs
- specs/test-standards.md integration tests
- specs/code-standards.md wrapper boundaries

## Wires up
- internal/docker daemon, image, copy, and exec wrapper confidence

## Acceptance
- Integration tests verify these wrapper success paths against a real Docker daemon
notes: Added integration-tagged Docker wrapper tests in internal/docker/integration_test.go covering daemon ping/info, image build+inspect+exists, copy-to/copy-from roundtrip, and non-interactive exec stdout/stderr/exit-code contract. Tests skip cleanly when daemon unavailable per spec.

title: Integration-test interactive attach and resize flow
description:
Add integration coverage for interactive attach behavior. ContainerAttach is the hardest Docker wrapper path because it proxies a live terminal session, resize events, and exit behavior; it needs dedicated PTY-based coverage.

## Scope
- Verify stdin and stdout proxying through an attached session
- Verify exit-code propagation
- Verify terminal resize handling and SIGWINCH-driven behavior

## Specs
- specs/test-standards.md integration tests
- specs/code-standards.md wrapper boundaries

## Wires up
- internal/docker ContainerAttach confidence

## Acceptance
- Integration tests exercise interactive attach behavior with a PTY against a real Docker daemon
notes: Added integration coverage for ContainerAttach interactive I/O and resize behavior in internal/docker/integration_test.go. New tests verify stdin/stdout proxying, exit-code propagation, and SIGWINCH-triggered terminal resize handling with a PTY-backed stdin.

title: Integration-test container wrapper success paths
description:
Add real-daemon integration coverage for the core container wrapper paths. These wrapper methods are the main translation boundary between havn and Docker, and today their success-path behavior is not verified against a live daemon.

## Scope
- Cover container create, start, stop, remove, list, and inspect success paths
- Verify response translation for names, status, labels, env, mounts, and networks
- Verify list-filter behavior against a real daemon

## Specs
- specs/test-standards.md integration tests
- specs/code-standards.md wrapper boundaries

## Wires up
- internal/docker container wrapper confidence

## Acceptance
- Integration tests verify the documented success-path behavior of the container wrapper against a real Docker daemon

title: Integration-test network and volume wrappers
description:
Add real-daemon integration coverage for network and volume wrapper behavior. These wrappers translate labels, filtering, and inspect responses, but currently only unit-level error paths are exercised.

## Scope
- Cover network create, inspect, and list success paths
- Cover volume create, inspect, and list success paths
- Verify prefix filtering, labels, and connected-container mapping against a real daemon

## Specs
- specs/test-standards.md integration tests
- specs/code-standards.md wrapper boundaries

## Wires up
- internal/docker network and volume wrapper confidence

## Acceptance
- Integration tests verify success-path mapping and filtering for network and volume wrappers against a real Docker daemon
notes: Added Docker integration coverage for network/volume wrappers: create+inspect success mapping, NamePrefix filtering contract, label filtering, and connected-container mapping against live daemon.

title: Specify port exposure model for SSH and project services
description:
Port exposure semantics are currently underspecified in two places: the root --port flag and the project-level ports config. Define one coherent contract for SSH and service port mappings so config, startup, and docs all describe the same behavior.

## Scope
- Specify what --port means
- Specify what project ports entries mean
- Define host-container mapping shape, defaults, and conflict behavior
- Clarify how SSH port exposure relates to general ports

## Specs
- specs/havn-overview.md global flags
- specs/havn-overview.md project config
- specs/cli-framework.md root flags

## Wires up
- Port-related config and startup documentation

## Acceptance
- The specs define a single coherent contract for --port and ports
notes: Specified coherent port exposure contract across specs: --port is SSH-only host port -> container 22, project ports uses explicit HOST:CONTAINER mappings, both merge into one Docker publish set, and startup fails on any host-port conflict with Docker error surfaced.

title: Specify project environment resolution semantics
description:
Project environment passthrough is documented only by example today. Specify the [environment] contract so users know how values are sourced, merged, and validated.

## Scope
- Define passthrough syntax such as ${VAR}
- Define behavior when host vars are unset
- Define merge and override behavior relative to havn-injected env vars

## Specs

Added CI guard test internal/ci/spec_contracts_test.go to prevent regressions in those spec statements.

title: Spec maintenance — fix contradictions and underspecified areas
description: Audit found 3 contradictions (Dolt enabled default, missing env var in lifecycle, drop confirmation wording) and 6 underspecified areas (--port/SSH, ports config, [environment] section, memory_swap, config source field). Fix contradictions in specs. Decide whether to fully specify or defer the underspecified areas.
notes: Resolved decisions: shared-Dolt contradictions fixed in specs; --port is SSH-only host-port-to-container-22; project ports are explicit host:container service mappings; ${VAR} copies host env and unset vars are validation errors; user env cannot override havn-managed runtime vars like SSH_AUTH_SOCK and BEADS_DOLT_*; memory_swap is config-only; config show source is a stable mirrored-shape contract.

title: Integration tests for Docker wrapper
description: Zero integration tests exist despite infrastructure being defined (Makefile target, build tag pattern documented). Docker wrapper success paths — type translation, response mapping, filter behavior, ContainerAttach interactive I/O — are completely unverified against a real daemon. Create //go:build integration tagged tests for internal/docker/ priority functions.
notes: Resolved decisions: integration tests should verify the wrapper contract, not only raw Docker behavior; NamePrefix uses wrapper-enforced prefix semantics consistently across resource types; interactive TTY attach uses a merged stream and does not promise separate stderr capture; resize handling remains best-effort; Docker-unavailable integration environments should skip cleanly with a clear message.

title: CI/CD pipeline
description: Quality gates (lint, test, build) run only locally via lefthook pre-commit hooks. No enforcement on push. Set up CI pipeline (GitHub Actions or equivalent) to enforce quality-gates.md requirements on every push and PR. Include unit tests, linting, build verification, and integration test stage.
notes: Resolved decisions: CI provider is GitHub Actions; the core CI contract is make check; integration tests run in a separate Docker-capable job via make test-integration; core checks should be merge-blocking immediately; integration should become merge-blocking once the Docker-backed job exists and is stable.
