title: Decide Docker create default working directory behavior
description:
Decide whether Docker container create options should set default `WorkingDir` to `ContainerProjectPath` in addition to explicit exec workdirs.

## Scope
- Determine whether default container `WorkingDir` is useful or unnecessary.
- If implemented, add create option and adapter wiring.
- If not implemented, document why explicit exec workdirs are sufficient.

## Specs
- `specs/project-container-runtime.md`
- `specs/environment-interface.md`

## Wires up
- Optional coherence improvement for unmanaged/default process behavior.

## Acceptance
- Decision is documented in code or commit message.
- Havn-managed exec correctness does not depend solely on Docker default workdir.
- If implemented, create options and Docker config use `/home/devuser/work/api`.

title: Decide doctor stance on app-port publishing diagnostics
description:
Decide whether havn doctor should report diagnostics for published app-port configuration, while staying app-agnostic and non-invasive. This is a decision artifact issue, not implementation by default.

## Context
- Service-port publishing guidance is now documented, including browser-access workflows.
- Current runtime port publishing can expose beyond loopback depending host/container networking; users may still benefit from diagnostics that explain effective publish wiring and likely misconfiguration.
- Doctor scope should remain transport/config-level and avoid app-specific probing.

## Decision to make
- Add a doctor check for app-port publishing wiring now, or
- Defer and document why/when to revisit.

## Scope
- Evaluate whether doctor should validate publish configuration wiring at transport/config level only
- Define boundaries that avoid app-protocol probing and avoid requiring services to be running
- If checks are recommended, define expected skip/fail semantics and output shape expectations
- If checks are not recommended now, record explicit defer rationale and revisit trigger

## Out of scope
- Application-level health checks
- Runtime probing of user services
- Any requirement to run notebook/app servers during doctor

## Specs
- specs/havn-doctor.md
- specs/configuration.md

## Acceptance
- Outcome is explicit: add check now or defer
- Rationale references doctor scope boundaries and user value
- If added, contract-level behavior is defined enough for implementation
- If deferred, concrete re-entry criteria are captured
notes: Decision outcome: defer adding doctor diagnostics for app-port publishing wiring for now. Rationale: doctor contract remains transport/config-level and app-agnostic; adding app-port diagnostics now risks implying runtime app reachability guarantees outside current doctor scope. Re-entry criteria: reopen only if repeated support incidents show users cannot distinguish service ports from SSH-only --port after docs/help updates, or a concrete transport-only check proposal is approved with stable skip/fail semantics.
close_reason: Deferred for now: keep doctor app-port diagnostics out of scope

title: Decide whether to add a service-port convenience flag
description:
Record a concrete product decision on whether havn should add a startup convenience flag (for example --publish host:container) for service-port mappings. This is a decision artifact issue, not an implementation issue.

## Context
- Current behavior already supports service publishing through config ports mappings (for example "2718:2718").
- Current behavior also keeps --port SSH-only, and docs/help now make that explicit.
- The remaining question is product ergonomics: whether adding a startup convenience flag materially improves first-run discoverability enough to justify API surface growth.

## Decision to make
- Keep config-only publishing (status quo), or
- Add a convenience startup flag for service ports.

## Scope
- Evaluate config-only workflow versus adding a convenience flag
- Assess discoverability, backward compatibility, and maintenance tradeoffs
- If accepted, define required semantics relative to existing ports behavior:
  - precedence and merge rules
  - validation and error behavior
  - interaction with existing --port (SSH-only) semantics
- Produce a recommendation and rationale maintainers can review quickly

## Out of scope
- Implementing any new flag
- Reverse proxy, auth, or app-specific runtime behavior

## Specs
- specs/cli-framework.md
- specs/configuration.md

## Acceptance
- Outcome is explicit: accepted or rejected
- Decision rationale covers UX value, compatibility impact, and complexity cost
- If accepted, required normative spec deltas are listed and implementation-ready
- If rejected, docs/help follow-up is stated clearly
notes: Decision outcome: reject adding a startup service publish flag (for example --publish) for now. Rationale: existing config ports already provides required capability with stable merge semantics; adding a new flag now increases CLI surface and precedence complexity relative to current user value. Re-entry criteria: reopen only if (1) >=3 independent user reports request ad-hoc publish UX after current docs/help updates, or (2) ongoing evidence of SSH --port misuse persists despite current guidance.
close_reason: Rejected for now: keep config-only service publishing via ports; revisit only with repeated evidence

title: Add deterministic doctor exit-0 contract test
description:
Add deterministic doctor pass-path coverage for exit-code semantics.

## Scope
- Add a stable all-pass fixture path for `havn doctor`
- Assert pass-path command exit behavior explicitly

## Specs
- specs/havn-doctor.md
- specs/cli-framework.md

## Wires up
- Closes finding F-009 by making doctor pass-path exit semantics explicitly enforced

## Acceptance
- All-pass doctor execution path deterministically asserts exit code 0
- Regression in pass-path exit semantics fails automated tests
acceptance_criteria: Doctor all-pass path is deterministically asserted to exit with code 0.
notes:
Priority intentionally lower than parent epic: this is a low-risk confidence-gap closure that does not block other typed-error coverage leaves.
Added deterministic doctor pass-path process-boundary coverage via fixture binary internal/cli/testdata/doctor_all_pass. New binary contract test asserts  exits 0 with all-pass conditions (global config present, host checks passing). Landed in commit bf1ee01.
Correction: the new binary contract test asserts the 'doctor' command exits 0 with all-pass conditions.

title: Finalize status-label transitions after evidence review
description: Perform final evidence-based status updates from Partial to Implemented for shared-Dolt infrastructure contracts and references only after prerequisite epics are complete and validated.
notes: Validated prerequisite epics are closed (havn-j39, havn-csk, havn-8ur, havn-7wj, havn-2ys, havn-04n) and finalized Partial->Implemented transitions for shared-Dolt surfaces: specs/shared-dolt-server.md status, specs/havn-doctor.md status, CLI support matrix rows in specs/cli-framework.md, and aligned derivative docs docs/cli-reference.md + docs/dolt-beads-guide.md.

title: Codify import/export as non-goals in authoritative specs
description:
Verify authoritative specs explicitly document `havn dolt import/export` as non-goals for shared-Dolt ownership, with brief rationale that migration semantics are owned by beads/Dolt workflows.

If any spec still implies havn-owned migration semantics, capture and resolve that drift.
notes: Updated authoritative specs to explicitly mark havn dolt import/export migration correctness semantics as non-goals for havn ownership (specs/shared-dolt-server.md, specs/cli-framework.md, specs/README.md).

title: Align support matrices with infrastructure-only Implemented status
description:
Verify support matrices express infrastructure-only Implemented status while `havn dolt import/export` are documented as non-goals for shared-Dolt ownership.

Ensure matrix wording avoids implying havn ownership of migration semantics.
notes: Updated support matrices to keep shared-Dolt infrastructure commands implemented while constraining havn dolt import/export to command-execution framing only; migration correctness semantics now explicitly labeled as beads/Dolt-owned in both specs/cli-framework.md and docs/cli-reference.md.

title: Align derivative docs to non-goal migration ownership statement
description:
Verify derivative docs consistently state import/export migration semantics are non-goals for havn and direct users to beads/Dolt responsibility boundaries with concise explanation.

Capture and resolve any doc drift that reintroduces ambiguous ownership language.
notes: Aligned derivative docs to explicitly state havn dolt import/export migration correctness is a non-goal for havn ownership and owned by beads/Dolt workflows. Updated docs/cli-reference.md and docs/dolt-beads-guide.md; verified with go test ./internal/cli -run TestDolt -count=1.

title: Define graduation acceptance checklist for shared-Dolt infrastructure
description: Create a single acceptance checklist for declaring shared-Dolt infrastructure Implemented, covering lifecycle, readiness, ownership, provisioning, diagnostics parity, output contract consistency, and observability requirements.
notes: Added authoritative Implemented graduation checklist in specs/shared-dolt-server.md covering lifecycle, readiness, ownership conflicts, provisioning env contract, diagnostics parity, output consistency, and observability semantics.

title: Shared-Dolt Partial-to-Implemented Graduation
description:
Reconcile specs, docs, and runtime behavior for shared-Dolt support and graduate infrastructure support from Partial to Implemented only when contract and implementation fully align.

This is a consolidation and acceptance epic:
- validate prerequisite epics for output consistency, diagnostics parity, reliability, observability fidelity, and migration-ownership boundary decisions
- codify `havn dolt import/export` as explicit non-goals for shared-Dolt ownership, with clear statement that migration semantics belong to beads/Dolt workflows
- remove stale partial caveats that conflict with the narrowed ownership boundary

Goal:
Produce a defensible Implemented state for shared-Dolt infrastructure with no spec-doc-runtime drift and no ambiguous migration ownership.

title: Define havn stop single-target success output contract
description:
Spec-first task under havn-ckz.

## Scope
- Update normative CLI spec text for havn stop single-target success output so behavior is explicit for name targets and path-like targets.
- Decide and document whether output should show raw input, resolved container name, or both.
- Clarify any JSON contract expectations for single-target stop (including whether container identity is included).

## Acceptance
- CLI spec documents the expected success output semantics for single-target stop.
- Spec text is unambiguous for path-like inputs such as dot and dot-slash forms.
- Derivative docs can align without conflicting interpretations.
notes: Defined stop single-target success contract in specs/cli-framework.md: human output must report resolved container identity (Stopped <container-name>) for both literal and path-like inputs; JSON success must include stable container field with resolved identity.

title: Add CLI regression coverage for stop single-target success output
description:
Regression task under havn-ckz.

## Scope
- Add CLI tests covering single-target stop success output for both literal container names and path-like targets.
- Cover at least one path-like input that resolves to a derived container name.
- If JSON output contract changes, add JSON assertions accordingly.

## Acceptance
- Tests verify success output semantics defined by the spec contract.
- Tests prevent regressions where raw path-like input is reported ambiguously.
- Existing stop test coverage remains green with no unrelated behavior changes.
notes: Added CLI regression coverage for single-target stop success output across literal and path-like inputs, including JSON assertions for stable "container" identity field.

title: Implement stop single-target output to match defined contract
description:
Implementation task under havn-ckz.

## Scope
- Update CLI stop single-target success output behavior to match the contract defined by the spec task.
- Preserve existing stop target resolution behavior.
- Keep multi-target stop all behavior unchanged unless contract says otherwise.

## Acceptance
- Single-target stop output is no longer ambiguous for path-like targets.
- Behavior matches the documented contract exactly.
- No regressions in stop command exit behavior.
notes: Implemented in commit a38366d: single-target stop now reports resolved container identity in human and JSON output; added/updated CLI and container stop coverage. Verified with go test ./internal/cli -run TestStopCommand -count=1.

title: Harden project-path normalization at container derivation boundary
description:
Preventative hardening task under havn-z2w.

## Scope
- Update the container-name derivation boundary used by startup and enter flows so relative project-path inputs are handled safely and consistently.
- Keep absolute-path behavior unchanged.
- Keep external command behavior unchanged.

## Acceptance
- Relative project-path inputs no longer fail at this boundary due to absolute-path assumptions.
- Absolute-path inputs continue to produce the same derived container names as today.
- Errors remain actionable when a path cannot be resolved.
notes: Normalized project-path input inside internal/container deriveContainerName by resolving to absolute clean paths before SplitProjectPath. Added regression test covering Enter with relative path '.' to lock boundary behavior used by startup/enter flows.

title: Add regression coverage for relative project-path handling in startup/enter
description:
Preventative regression test task under havn-z2w.

## Scope
- Add domain-level regression coverage for representative relative inputs at the startup and enter derivation boundary.
- Cover at least: . , ./project , ../project (with deterministic expectations in test fixtures).
- Verify absolute-path behavior remains intact.

## Acceptance
- Tests fail on pre-hardening behavior and pass after boundary hardening.
- Coverage demonstrates no regression for existing absolute-path cases.
- Test expectations are deterministic and command-agnostic.
notes: Added regression coverage for startup and enter derivation with ., ./project, and ../project relative inputs using deterministic temp-dir fixtures; existing absolute-path startup/enter coverage remains green.

title: havn stop success output is ambiguous for path targets
description:
havn stop currently prints "Stopped <target>" using the raw CLI argument, not the resolved container name. For path-like targets this produces misleading output (for example "Stopped ."), even though a container with a derived name was actually stopped.

## Scope
- Emit success status using resolved container identity for stop single-target path inputs.
- Keep literal name behavior unchanged.
- Add CLI regression coverage for path-like success output.

## Why bug
- User-visible output is incorrect/ambiguous for valid path-target workflows.
- Closely related to the path-vs-name resolution class in havn-0dg.

## Acceptance
- Path-like stop commands show an unambiguous stopped container identifier.
- Literal name inputs continue to report expected output.
notes: Verified fixed on main: stop single-target status now uses resolved container name from container.Stop, with CLI regression coverage for literal and path targets (including '.' path). Confirmed via go test ./internal/cli -run TestStopCommand_ -count=1.

title: Guard container name derivation against relative project paths
description:
internal/container deriveContainerName currently calls name.SplitProjectPath directly and assumes projectPath is absolute. Current CLI call paths normalize to absolute paths first, but the domain helper is brittle: any future caller that passes relative input (for example . or ./repo) will fail with "path must be absolute" and reproduce the same path-vs-literal class of bug seen in havn stop.

## Scope
- Normalize project paths inside derivation boundary (or enforce with explicit contract plus early validation).
- Add regression tests for relative path inputs (. , ./x , ../x) against derivation/call boundary.
- Keep existing absolute-path behavior unchanged.

## Why bug
- This is a latent correctness bug in a shared domain helper, not just missing docs/tests.
- It can surface as soon as a caller bypasses CLI normalization.

## Acceptance
- Relative path inputs no longer fail due to absolute-path assumptions at this boundary.
- Tests lock in behavior so path-like regressions cannot silently reappear.
notes: Added regression coverage in internal/container/stop_test.go for ../project path input (TestStop_DotDotPathArgDerivesContainerName), complementing existing startup/enter . ./ ../ coverage. Verified with go test ./internal/container and pre-commit suite; pushed in 5fdb539.

title: remove markdown-prose assertions from repository CI tests
description:
The current internal/ci suite contains tests that read markdown files and assert exact headings or sentences. Remove those assertions where the file content is being used as a brittle test oracle rather than as a stable machine-readable artifact.

## Scope
- audit internal/ci/spec_contracts_test.go for direct prose assertions against specs/*.md, README.md, and docs/*.md
- remove checks that depend on exact wording or heading names
- retain only checks that survive editorial rewrites under the new policy

## Specs
- specs/test-standards.md Section 5

## Wires up
- repository CI contract tests

## Acceptance
- internal/ci no longer contains required tests whose pass/fail depends on exact markdown prose
- editorial-only rewrites to specs or docs do not break repository tests
notes: Added repository CI policy test in internal/ci that scans *_test.go and fails when tests reference specs/, README.md, or docs/ markdown paths as assertion oracles. This prevents reintroducing prose-coupled CI checks.

title: remove spec and docs files as direct test oracles
description:
Repository tests currently read specs/*.md, README.md, and docs/*.md and assert exact text. That makes harmless editorial changes fail CI and turns human-readable documentation into a brittle test fixture. This epic removes that dependency and re-centers verification on runtime behavior, machine-readable contracts, and narrowly justified structural checks.

## Scope
- remove required tests that treat markdown prose as a source of executable truth
- preserve actual contract coverage through runtime, CLI, config, and repository-structure verification
- codify the testing boundary so prose-coupled checks do not return

## Specs
- specs/test-standards.md Section 5
- specs/README.md Spec Governance
- specs/havn-overview.md User-Facing Docs

## Wires up
- internal/ci repository contract tests
- authoritative specs in specs/
- derivative docs in README.md and docs/

## Acceptance
- required tests do not fail because spec or doc prose was reworded
- contract verification is performed through code behavior, machine-readable outputs, or narrowly scoped structural checks
- project standards explicitly forbid prose-coupled repository tests

title: Stop doctor --all from falling back to the wrong config for target containers
description:
`havn doctor --all` can currently fall back to the outer/default config when resolving effective config for a target container fails. That can make per-container diagnostics describe the wrong intended state. This task makes `doctor --all` truthful for each target container it inspects.

## Scope
- stop per-container doctor runs from silently reusing the wrong config after target-specific resolution failure
- keep target-specific config-resolution failures visible in doctor output
- ensure downstream checks only run when the target container's required effective config is actually available

## Specs
- specs/havn-doctor.md §33
- specs/havn-doctor.md §121

## Wires up
- per-container doctor results that correspond to the target container's intended config

## Acceptance
- `havn doctor --all` does not silently substitute another container's or the outer config when target-specific config resolution fails
- target-specific config-resolution failures remain visible and actionable in doctor output
close_reason: Completed: doctor --all now surfaces target-specific config-resolution failures as container-tier errors and skips downstream per-target checks instead of falling back to the wrong config

title: Make havn dolt status report runtime port faithfully or narrow contract
description:
The shared-Dolt status surface includes the runtime port, but the production adapter does not currently populate it. This task reconciles status output with actual available runtime information so the status surface you use is trustworthy.

## Scope
- reconcile status output with actual available runtime-port information
- preserve stable status semantics as far as the supported contract allows
- narrow the published contract if faithful runtime-port reporting is not actually supported

## Out of scope
- unrelated status-output redesign
- adding new runtime metadata beyond the existing status contract
acceptance_criteria:
- `havn dolt status` and the published contract agree on whether and how runtime port is reported
- if runtime port is reported, the reported value is faithful on supported paths
notes:
Narrowed status contract to faithful runtime fields: removed runtime port from  payload/output, updated shared-dolt spec and derivative docs, and added spec-contract test coverage (commit b80232f).
Implementation landed in commit b80232f: status output now excludes runtime port and reports only faithful runtime fields; shared-dolt spec and guide updated to match.

title: Add root-command coverage for remaining runtime startup flags
description:
The root command accepts runtime flags that materially change startup behavior, but direct CLI-boundary confidence is still incomplete. Some behavior is already proven, but several remaining user-facing runtime flags still lack root-command coverage where users actually invoke them.

## Scope
- add direct root-command coverage for runtime flags whose startup behavior is not already directly proven
- assert resulting startup behavior at the CLI boundary, not just lower-level helper behavior

## Out of scope
- duplicating boundary coverage for flags that are already directly proven
- lower-level unit coverage that does not improve the root-command contract
acceptance_criteria:
- root-command tests cover the remaining user-facing runtime flags whose behavior is not already directly proven
- the tests demonstrate that those flags affect resolved startup behavior as documented
notes: Added direct root-command startup coverage for --shell, --env, --cpus, --memory, --port, --image, and --no-dolt by asserting resolved config passed to StartOrAttach.

title: Add root-command coverage for Dolt-enabled startup setup and injected env
description:
Lower layers already cover parts of Dolt setup and env injection, but the root command still lacks direct boundary coverage for the Dolt-enabled startup path you actually use. This task adds that missing proof at the user-facing CLI boundary.

## Scope
- cover Dolt-enabled root-command startup through shared-Dolt setup
- verify the injected beads/shared-Dolt env contract on the actual root-command path
- build on existing lower-level confidence rather than duplicating it mechanically

## Out of scope
- broad end-to-end expansion beyond the user-facing startup boundary
- retesting lower-level helpers solely for duplication
acceptance_criteria:
- CLI-boundary tests prove that Dolt-enabled root-command startup performs shared-Dolt setup
- CLI-boundary tests prove that the expected beads/shared-Dolt env vars are injected on that startup path
notes: Added CLI-boundary root startup coverage for Dolt-enabled path by exercising root RunE through StartService+container startup composition. New test verifies shared Dolt setup is invoked and BEADS_DOLT_* env contract is injected into container create opts, including default database name resolution from project path.

title: Resolve doctor stable-surface drift for container_tier and first-run severity
description:
Doctor currently emits an undocumented `container_tier` check and warns on some valid first-run states that the spec describes as non-alarming. This task reconciles those stable-surface mismatches so doctor is less noisy and more contract-accurate.

## Scope
- decide whether `container_tier` belongs in the published doctor surface
- align first-run warning severity with the documented non-alarming intent for valid missing resources/config

## Specs
- specs/havn-doctor.md §85
- specs/havn-doctor.md §197
- specs/configuration.md §52

## Wires up
- less surprising doctor output
- published doctor surface that matches emitted checks and severities

## Acceptance
- the doctor check catalog and emitted checks agree
- valid first-run states are not surfaced as unexpectedly alarming warnings
close_reason: Completed: first-run missing base image/network/volumes now report skip instead of alarming warnings, and container_tier is now part of the published doctor check catalog/docs

title: Add missing CLI-boundary proof for the personal startup path
description:
The implementation is close and lower-level coverage already exists in several places, but your real workflow still lacks direct proof that the root command drives the personal Dolt-enabled startup path correctly. This epic adds the missing CLI-boundary proof without turning it into a broad testing rewrite.

## Scope
- add direct root-command coverage for runtime flags that still lack boundary tests
- add direct root-command coverage for the Dolt-enabled startup path, including shared-Dolt setup and injected env
- preserve the current CLI surface while increasing confidence in the path you actually use

## Out of scope
- broad test refactors that do not improve user-facing contract coverage
- duplicating lower-level coverage that already exists outside the CLI boundary
acceptance_criteria:
- root-command coverage proves the remaining runtime flags affect startup behavior as documented
- root-command coverage proves the Dolt-enabled startup path performs shared-Dolt setup and injects the expected env on the user-facing CLI path
notes: Added root CLI boundary coverage proving --config overrides default global config path for startup resolution; this complements existing root startup tests for runtime flags and Dolt-enabled BEADS env injection.

title: Align contributor guidance with actual merge-gate requirements
description:
Update contributor-facing guidance so local expectations and required merge behavior match the actual enforced quality-gate contract.

## Scope
- contributor guidance for required local checks
- explanation of when integration tests are expected relative to branch protection
- local workflow wording for quality-gate preparation
- removal of wording that understates enforced merge requirements

## Specs
- specs/quality-gates.md
- specs/test-standards.md

## Wires up
- contributor documentation that accurately reflects enforced merge expectations

## Acceptance
- contributor guidance matches the quality gates that actually protect `main`
- local workflow docs do not understate required merge-time checks
- contributors can determine the expected quality-gate workflow from docs without contradiction
notes: Updated CONTRIBUTING quality-gate guidance to explicitly include make test-boundary-confidence and state that integration-tests and boundary-confidence are required merge checks on main; added CI spec contract assertion to keep this requirement from regressing.

title: Add structural checks for quality-gate parity across docs and automation
description:
Expand structural regression coverage so drift between docs, hooks, Makefile targets, CI workflow, and branch-protection expectations is caught automatically.

## Scope
- structural tests for quality-gate parity
- automated checks for key docs/automation alignment points
- regression protection for workflow and branch-protection contract drift
- parity checks for documented-vs-enforced quality expectations

## Specs
- specs/quality-gates.md
- specs/test-standards.md

## Wires up
- stronger automated protection for quality-gate parity over time

## Acceptance
- structural tests cover more than workflow/job existence
- docs/automation parity regressions are more likely to be caught automatically
- quality-gate alignment is enforced as a maintained contract rather than a one-time cleanup
notes: Added structural parity contract test that enforces required merge checks list includes quality-gates/integration-tests/boundary-confidence, and aligned specs/quality-gates.md plus CONTRIBUTING.md wording with .github/settings.yml branch protection.

title: Make formatting gates validate the committed tree consistently
description:
Bring formatting enforcement into parity so local and CI quality gates validate the same tree rather than silently rewriting code before checking it.

## Scope
- formatting gate behavior across Makefile, hooks, and CI
- committed-tree vs rewritten-tree validation semantics
- parity between local and CI formatting enforcement
- alignment with the documented formatting contract

## Specs
- specs/quality-gates.md

## Wires up
- trustworthy formatting enforcement that means the same thing locally and in CI

## Acceptance
- formatting gates no longer rely on silently rewriting code before validation
- local and CI formatting enforcement reflect the same contract
- the documented formatting gate matches what automation actually enforces
notes: Implemented committed-tree formatting validation by adding make fmt-check (gofmt + gci list), wiring lefthook fmt job to it, and updating quality-gates spec + CI contract test coverage.

title: Align documented build and test gate requirements with enforcement
description:
Reconcile the documented build/test merge requirements with what hooks, Makefile targets, and CI actually enforce so contributors and branch protection are working from one coherent definition.

## Scope
- build-gate requirement alignment across specs, docs, Makefile, hooks, and CI
- test-gate requirement alignment across local and CI workflows
- consistency of merge-gate terminology and expectations
- resolution of conflicts between normative quality-gate sources

## Specs
- specs/quality-gates.md
- specs/test-standards.md

## Wires up
- one coherent definition of build and test quality gates

## Acceptance
- build and test requirements are documented consistently across the authoritative sources
- automation reflects the same build/test gate definition that the docs describe
- contributors and CI no longer operate from conflicting merge-gate expectations
notes: Aligned merge-gate requirements with actual enforcement: updated specs/test-standards.md required checks to match make/CI jobs and updated specs/quality-gates.md to state integration-tests and boundary-confidence are required merge checks on main. Added internal/ci spec contract test TestSpecs_BuildAndTestMergeGateRequirementsMatchEnforcement to prevent future drift.

title: Tighten CI and local quality-gate parity
description:
Bring local hooks, docs, and CI closer to the same enforcement model so merge confidence matches the documented workflow. This epic focuses on reliable, predictable quality gates rather than feature behavior.

## Scope
- local vs CI parity
- merge-gate fidelity
- formatting/lint/build/test consistency
- documented vs enforced quality expectations

## Specs
- specs/quality-gates.md
- specs/test-standards.md

## Wires up
- more trustworthy contributor and CI validation flow

## Acceptance
- documented quality gates match what CI actually enforces
- local workflows more closely match CI outcomes
- merge validation reflects the real checked tree and required checks

title: Decide first logger DI boundaries
description:
Logger DI is wired only at the CLI root today. Decide where the first real logging boundaries should be so logger plumbing and call sites land intentionally rather than spreading into every package at once.

## Scope
- Pick the first domain or wrapper packages that should receive a logger
- Define the logging boundary relative to CLI status output and returned errors
- Keep the decision aligned with slog and dependency-injection rules

## Specs
- specs/code-standards.md logging
- specs/architecture-principles.md observability
- specs/cli-framework.md dependency injection

## Wires up
- Future logger constructor parameters and call sites

## Acceptance
- The repo has an explicit first-pass logging boundary policy for domain and wrapper packages
notes: Decided first-pass logger DI boundary: inject loggers first into internal/docker wrapper only; keep domain packages logger-free initially; preserve explicit CLI status output as separate from diagnostic logs.

title: Plumb slog logger into first chosen packages
description:
Plumb slog logger dependencies into the first chosen package set and add the first structured log call sites there. This turns the existing CLI-root logger setup into real domain-level observability without adopting slog.Default or duplicating user-facing status output.

## Scope
- Add *slog.Logger constructor parameters where the policy calls for them
- Propagate logger values from CLI production wiring
- Add the first structured log calls using the documented attr conventions

## Specs
- specs/code-standards.md logging
- specs/cli-framework.md dependency injection

## Wires up
- Domain or wrapper packages selected by the logging-boundary decision

## Acceptance
- At least one non-CLI package receives a logger via DI and emits structured logs according to the repo logging rules
notes: Wired logger DI from CLI root into internal/docker by adding Client.SetLogger and propagating deps.Logger in PersistentPreRunE. Added first structured wrapper logs at docker daemon boundary (operations ping/info with component=docker) and tests covering injected logger propagation + structured operation attrs.

title: Add compile-time assertions for container-facing implementations
description:
Add compile-time assertions for the concrete implementations behind the container-facing interfaces once their implementor strategy is settled. This catches interface drift at the implementation boundary instead of at call sites.

## Scope
- Add assertions for the container startup, build, list, and stop interfaces
- Keep assertions next to the real concrete implementations

## Specs
- specs/code-standards.md compile-time interface assertions

## Wires up
- internal/container consumer-defined interfaces

## Acceptance
- The concrete implementations behind container-facing interfaces are asserted at compile time

title: Add compile-time assertions for doctor, volume, and Dolt implementations
description:
Add compile-time assertions for the concrete implementations behind the doctor, volume, and Dolt-facing interfaces once their implementor strategy is settled. This extends the same hardening pattern across the rest of the Docker-backed boundary packages.

## Scope
- Add assertions for doctor, volume, and dolt consumer interfaces
- Keep assertions next to the real concrete implementations

## Specs
- specs/code-standards.md compile-time interface assertions

## Wires up
- internal/doctor, internal/volume, and internal/dolt consumer-defined interfaces

## Acceptance
- The concrete implementations behind doctor, volume, and dolt-facing interfaces are asserted at compile time
notes: Added pointer-form compile-time assertions for cli dockerDoctorBackend, dockerVolumeBackend, and dockerDoltBackend; added spec contract test in internal/ci/spec_contracts_test.go to lock this implementor assertion pattern.

title: Clarify concrete implementors for interface assertions
description:
Before adding compile-time interface assertions, clarify which concrete types are supposed to satisfy the consumer-defined interfaces across container, volume, dolt, and doctor. The current docker wrapper signatures suggest missing adapter types or a stale assumption in the epic description.

## Scope
- Identify the concrete implementation boundary for consumer-defined Docker-adjacent interfaces
- Decide whether assertions belong on internal/docker.Client, dedicated adapters, or other concrete types
- Keep the result aligned with the consumer-owns-interface pattern

## Specs
- specs/code-standards.md consumer-defined interfaces
- specs/architecture-principles.md dependency isolation

## Wires up
- Future compile-time assertions for Docker-backed implementations

## Acceptance
- The repo has a clear concrete-implementor strategy for each consumer-defined interface that is supposed to gain a compile-time assertion
notes: Concrete implementor strategy documented in specs/code-standards.md: container.Backend/container.StopBackend -> cli.dockerContainerBackend, doctor.Backend -> cli.dockerDoctorBackend, volume.Backend -> cli.dockerVolumeBackend, dolt.Backend -> cli.dockerDoltBackend. Added spec contract test to lock this mapping and clarify assertions belong on adapters when interfaces are implemented via cli composition rather than docker.Client directly.

title: Write doctor troubleshooting guide
description:
Document havn doctor as the main troubleshooting entrypoint. Users need a guide to what doctor checks, how to read results, and what to do next when checks fail.

## Scope
- Doctor command purpose and flags
- Check tiers, exit codes, and output modes
- Common troubleshooting flows linked to doctor results

## Specs
- specs/havn-doctor.md
- specs/havn-overview.md Diagnostics

## Wires up
- User-facing troubleshooting documentation

## Acceptance
- Users can understand and act on havn doctor output without reading the implementation spec
notes: Added user-facing doctor troubleshooting guide at docs/doctor-troubleshooting.md and linked it from README troubleshooting section. Added CI spec-contract tests to keep guide coverage and README link from regressing.

title: Write Dolt and beads user guide
description:
Document the shared Dolt and beads model for users working with databases in havn. This should explain the architecture, operational commands, migration workflows, and any current implementation caveats.

## Scope
- Shared Dolt server model
- Operational command reference
- Import and export workflows
- Beads integration and current-status caveats

## Specs
- specs/shared-dolt-server.md
- specs/havn-overview.md Dolt Integration

## Wires up
- User-facing Dolt and beads documentation

## Acceptance
- Users can understand the shared Dolt model and the supported workflows from project docs
notes: Added docs/dolt-beads-guide.md with shared-server model, operational command reference, import/export workflows, beads integration environment variables, and current caveats. Linked from README Guides.

title: Write CONTRIBUTING guide for developers
description:
Human contributors need a single onboarding document for local setup and workflow. Create CONTRIBUTING.md with the repo-specific development, testing, and issue-tracking flow.

## Scope
- Local dev setup and prerequisites
- make targets, lint, and test commands
- Repository structure overview
- PR and bd workflow for contributors

## Specs
- specs/quality-gates.md
- specs/code-standards.md
- AGENTS.md

## Wires up
- Contributor onboarding

## Acceptance
- CONTRIBUTING.md explains how to work on the repo as a contributor without reverse-engineering the workflow from specs and hooks
notes: Added CONTRIBUTING.md covering prerequisites, local setup, make quality gates, repository structure, bd workflow, and PR workflow; added internal/ci contract test to enforce required sections.

title: Write user-facing CLI reference
description:
The product needs a user-facing command reference that matches the actual CLI surface and clearly distinguishes implemented behavior from planned behavior while the CLI is still incomplete.

## Scope
- Document root command, subcommands, and global flags
- Document output modes and JSON conventions
- Include an explicit support matrix or caveats for unimplemented commands

## Specs
- specs/havn-overview.md CLI Interface and JSON output
- specs/cli-framework.md command tree and output modes

## Wires up
- User-facing CLI reference

## Acceptance
- Users can look up command and flag behavior from project docs without relying only on --help output
notes: Added docs/cli-reference.md with command tree, global flags, output/JSON conventions, and an explicit implemented-vs-planned support matrix; linked it from README; added CI contract coverage in internal/ci/spec_contracts_test.go.

title: Write configuration guide
description:
The configuration model is rich enough to need its own guide. Document config file locations, precedence, examples, and how to inspect the effective config with havn config show.

## Scope
- Global and project config files
- Env var and flag precedence
- Example TOML and common configuration patterns
- config show usage and output interpretation

## Specs
- specs/havn-overview.md Configuration
- specs/havn-overview.md config show JSON

## Wires up
- User-facing configuration documentation

## Acceptance
- Users can configure havn and understand the effective config output from project docs alone
notes: Added docs/configuration-guide.md covering config file locations, precedence rules (including env/flake resolution), practical TOML examples, and havn config show output interpretation. Linked guide from README guides section.

title: Write README with overview and quickstart
description:
The README is currently empty. Write a user-facing README that explains what havn is, how to install or build it today, how to get started, and where to find more detailed docs.

## Scope
- Project overview and prerequisites
- Current installation or build paths
- Basic quickstart and current-status caveats
- Links to deeper documentation

## Specs
- specs/havn-overview.md Overview and CLI Interface
- specs/cli-framework.md command tree and output modes

## Wires up
- Top-level user onboarding

## Acceptance
- README gives new users enough information to understand and try the project without reading the specs first

title: User-facing documentation
description: README is empty, no user-facing docs, no CONTRIBUTING.md. Write: README with project overview, installation, usage examples; CONTRIBUTING.md with dev setup, architecture overview, PR workflow; and any user-facing command documentation beyond --help output.
notes: Resolved decisions: user-facing docs are implementation-first; quickstart and installation should describe the current Go/Make workflow; planned/spec-defined behavior must be labeled clearly as planned; CONTRIBUTING should stay human-focused and reference bd workflow without copying AGENTS.md wholesale; CLI docs should include an explicit support matrix while commands remain partially implemented.

title: Code hardening — compile-time assertions and logger DI
description: Two spec-required patterns not yet implemented: (1) Compile-time interface assertions (var _ Interface = (*Type)(nil)) missing in internal/docker/ for all 9+ consumer-defined interfaces — interface drift only caught at call sites. (2) Logger DI infrastructure is ready (Deps.Logger wired) but no domain package receives or uses a logger yet. Wire when logging calls are needed.
notes: Resolved decisions: compile-time assertions belong on the actual concrete implementor or adapter for each consumer-defined interface, not automatically on docker.Client; the implementor strategy task remains first; logger DI should start narrowly at long-lived external-system boundaries; explicit user status output and structured logs are separate channels and should not be conflated.

title: Assess whether current tooling dependency weight is justified by active quality gates
description:
The runtime dependency surface is modest, but the repo still carries a large transitive toolchain graph relative to the small active linter set. This task evaluates whether that dependency weight is justified by the current quality gates and documents the answer without assuming easy removals.

## Scope
- review active tooling requirements against current lint and formatting configuration
- determine whether the current transitive toolchain surface is justified
- identify any safe reductions that do not weaken intended quality gates

## Out of scope
- changing user-facing CLI behavior
- broad tooling redesign unrelated to actual dependency justification or safe reduction
acceptance_criteria:
- the repo has a documented decision on whether the current transitive toolchain surface is justified
- any safe dependency/tooling reductions are identified or eliminated without unintentionally weakening active quality gates
notes: Documented quality-gate toolchain decision in specs/quality-gates.md: current dependency surface is justified by active gates; no safe reduction accepted unless merge gates, lint orchestration contract, or equivalent built-in Go checks change. Added CI spec-contract test coverage in internal/ci/spec_contracts_test.go to keep decision explicit.

title: Split CLI Docker adapters by domain concern
description:
internal/cli/adapters.go currently mixes startup wiring, doctor adapters, Dolt adapters, port checking, mount resolution, tar helpers, and exec error handling in one large file. This task separates those adapters by domain concern so the CLI boundary remains thin and dependency translation stays easier to locate and evolve.

## Scope
- split Docker-backed adapter implementations into smaller files organized by concern
- keep existing contracts between internal/cli, internal/docker, internal/container, internal/doctor, internal/dolt, and internal/volume
- preserve current runtime behavior

## Specs
- specs/architecture-principles.md §1
- specs/architecture-principles.md §4
- specs/architecture-principles.md §7
- specs/cli-framework.md §153

## Wires up
- clearer dependency-wrapper boundaries for start, doctor, Dolt, and volume flows

## Acceptance
- Docker adapter code is organized by domain concern instead of one catch-all file
- CLI code can locate the relevant runtime adapter without scanning unrelated adapter logic
- no published CLI or JSON behavior changes
notes: Split CLI docker adapters into domain-focused files (start, container, doctor, dolt, volume, and shared exec helpers) while keeping compile-time assertions in internal/cli/adapters.go for contract checks.

title: Extract only mechanical shared helpers from Dolt CLI commands
description:
`internal/cli/dolt.go` contains repeated command scaffolding for config resolution, action-result shaping, output rendering, and prefixed error handling. This task reduces mechanical repetition while keeping the explicit command tree and command-local behavior easy to read.

## Scope
- extract shared helpers only where repetition is mechanical
- preserve command names, flags, output behavior, and error surfaces
- keep parent/child command structure explicit and easy to audit

## Out of scope
- abstracting command-local behavior that is clearer when left inline
- large command-framework refactors unrelated to actual repetition
acceptance_criteria:
- Dolt subcommands remain reachable at the same CLI paths with the same flags
- repeated mechanical command plumbing in `internal/cli/dolt.go` is reduced
- JSON and human-readable output contracts remain unchanged
notes: Extracted shared Dolt CLI command plumbing helpers in internal/cli/dolt.go (manager guard, prefixed error wrapper, config resolution, and JSON ok response) while preserving command paths/flags/output behavior.

title: Reduce repetitive doctor check type boilerplate while keeping the check catalog explicit
description:
The doctor package already has builder extraction for assembling check lists, but many individual check types still repeat the same structural boilerplate. This task trims that remaining repetition without hiding stable check identifiers, prerequisite behavior, or the published check catalog.

## Scope
- reduce repeated per-check type boilerplate in `internal/doctor`
- preserve stable check identifiers, tiers, prerequisite semantics, and output fields
- keep the check catalog explicit and easy to audit

## Out of scope
- redoing list-builder extraction that is already in place
- abstractions that make the stable check surface harder to inspect
acceptance_criteria:
- doctor check IDs and behavior remain unchanged
- doctor check definitions contain less repetitive structural boilerplate
- the published doctor output and prerequisite/skip behavior stay intact
notes: Consolidated host/container check metadata into shared checkMetadata constructors while keeping stable IDs and explicit check catalog in builders; added metadata defensive-copy test and verified via make check.

title: Finish slimming oversized CLI and runtime boundary files
description:
The repo is not broadly oversized, but several boundary files still carry mixed concerns or mechanical repetition beyond what the architecture principles call for. This epic tracks focused simplification work to make the codebase easier to navigate without changing the published CLI or runtime contracts.

## Scope
- reduce local code bloat in oversized CLI and doctor boundary files
- preserve the current command surface and published behavior
- keep refactors aligned with package boundaries and dependency-isolation rules

## Out of scope
- user-facing behavior changes
- architecture churn that does not materially improve code locality or readability
acceptance_criteria:
- oversized boundary code is decomposed into clearer units without changing the published CLI contract
- Docker wrapper code, Dolt CLI command code, and doctor check definitions better match the repo’s compositional architecture

title: TEST blocker

value: Dolt integration: per-container is the default; shared Dolt server is opt-in. Primary use case is beads issue tracking inside the container.
Section 5 of specs/test-standards.md now forbids prose-coupled markdown assertions in required tests; use behavior, machine-readable output, typed errors, or stable structural invariants instead.
compile-time assertions (var _ Interface = (*Type)(nil)) are missing in internal/docker/ for all 9 consumer-defined interfaces — the only code-standards §4 violation found in the audit
AGENTS.md references docs/QUICKSTART.md which does not exist — stale link from earlier project state
container.Backend and container.Info are the exported names (not ContainerBackend/ContainerInfo) — revive flags Go stutter when type name repeats the package name

Environment interface contract v1 ratification epic havn-4fd is closed at Status Partial; apps.<system>.havn-session-prepare capability name is reserved/stable and remaining work is runtime alignment only.

All 12 CLI stub commands have complete domain implementations in internal/. The gap is exclusively CLI wiring — connecting Deps fields to domain constructors in Execute() and writing RunE functions. DoctorBackend and VolumeManager are declared in Deps but never initialized. DoltManager is missing from Deps entirely.

Nix registry startup prep preserves existing state aliases and only imports missing aliases from legacy ~/.config/nix/registry.json into ~/.local/state/nix/registry.json; malformed JSON surfaces actionable parse guidance.

havn runs on the host (not inside a container). One container per project directory: 'havn .' starts or attaches. Base image is minimal Ubuntu + Nix + devuser; all tooling comes from 'nix develop' at runtime, not baked into the image. Shared Nix store volume across containers; XDG volumes (data/cache/state) also shared. This separation is the whole point: Docker is the box, Nix owns the environment, havn manages lifecycle.
